narrative:
"Listen, all ye who have just now heard the laws about Gods, and about our dear forefathers: Of all the things which a man has, next to the Gods, his soul is the most divine and most truly his own. Now in every man there are two parts: the better and superior, which rules, and the worse and inferior, which serves; and the ruling part of him is always to be preferred to the subject. Wherefore I am right in bidding every one next to the Gods, who are our masters, and those who in order follow them [i.e., the demons], to honour his own soul, which every one seems to honour, but no one honours as he ought; for honour is a divine good, and no evil thing is honourable; and he who thinks that he can honour the soul by word or gift, or any sort of compliance, without making her in any way better, seems to honour her, but honours her not at all.

"For example, every man, from his very boyhood, fancies that he is able to know everything, and thinks that he honours his soul by praising her, and he is very ready to let her do whatever she may like. But I mean to say that in acting thus he injures his soul, and is far from honouring her; whereas, in our opinion, he ought to honour her as second only to the Gods."
refashioned:
The Athenian addresses those who have already heard the laws concerning the gods and their ancestors.

Of everything a person possesses, after the gods themselves, the soul is the most divine and most truly one's own. Within every person there are two elements: a higher part that rules and a lower part that serves. The ruling element must always be given priority over the subordinate one. This is why it is right to urge everyone, after honoring the gods who are our masters and the divine beings who follow them, to honor their own soul. Everyone believes they honor their soul, but no one honors it as they should. True honor is a divine good, and nothing evil can be honorable. Whoever imagines they can honor the soul through flattering words, material gifts, or indulgent compliance -- without actually making it better -- only appears to honor it while doing nothing of the sort.

Consider how from boyhood onward, every person imagines they already know everything and believes they honor their soul by praising it, readily allowing it to do whatever it pleases. But in truth, such behavior injures the soul rather than honoring it. The soul deserves to be honored as second only to the gods themselves.
elegant: After the gods, a person's soul is their most divine possession. Every person contains a higher ruling part and a lower serving part; the higher must always take precedence. Yet no one truly honors their soul as they should. Flattery, gifts, and indulgence without moral improvement are false honor. From boyhood we praise our own souls and let them run unchecked, believing this is respect -- when in truth it is injury. The soul deserves honor second only to the gods, and that honor consists in making it better.
summary: The Athenian declares that the soul is a person's most divine possession after the gods. True honor of the soul requires making it better, not flattering or indulging it. From childhood, people mistake self-praise and permissiveness for respect toward their own souls.
distilled: Truly honoring your soul means improving it, not indulging it -- flattery and permissiveness are disguised forms of injury.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, continuing a public address (the preamble to the laws). This passage establishes the soul-body hierarchy central to Platonic ethics -- cf. Republic IV (tripartite soul) and Phaedrus 246a-249d (the chariot allegory). The distinction between the ruling and serving parts within a person echoes Republic 430e-431a on self-mastery. The claim that no one honors the soul properly parallels Socrates' mission in the Apology (29d-30b) to exhort Athenians to care for their souls above wealth or reputation. The 'demons' (daimones) referenced here are intermediary divine beings discussed in the Symposium (202d-203a).

narrative:
"Again, when a man thinks that others are to be blamed, and not himself, for the errors which he has committed from time to time, and the many and great evils which befell him in consequence, and is always fancying himself to be exempt and innocent, he is under the idea that he is honouring his soul; whereas the very reverse is the fact, for he is really injuring her.

"And when, disregarding the word and approval of the legislator, he indulges in pleasure, then again he is far from honouring her; he only dishonours her, and fills her full of evil and remorse; or when he does not endure to the end the labours and fears and sorrows and pains which the legislator approves, but gives way before them, then, by yielding, he does not honour the soul, but by all such conduct he makes her to be dishonourable; nor when he thinks that life at any price is a good, does he honour her, but yet once more he dishonours her; for the soul having a notion that the world below is all evil, he yields to her, and does not resist and teach or convince her that, for aught she knows, the world of the Gods below, instead of being evil, may be the greatest of all goods."
refashioned:
Furthermore, when a person blames others rather than themselves for their own repeated mistakes and the great misfortunes that follow, imagining themselves blameless and innocent, they believe they are honoring their soul. But the opposite is true -- they are actually harming it.

When someone ignores the guidance and standards of the lawgiver to chase pleasure, they are not honoring their soul but degrading it, filling it with corruption and regret. When they cannot endure to the end the hardships, fears, sorrows, and pains that the lawgiver deems necessary, but instead surrender before them, this yielding does not honor the soul -- it makes it dishonorable through and through.

Nor does a person honor their soul by clinging to life at any cost. This too is a form of dishonor. When the soul harbors the belief that the afterlife is entirely evil, the person who simply yields to this fear -- rather than challenging and teaching the soul that the realm of the gods below might in fact be the greatest of all blessings -- dishonors it once again.
elegant: Blaming others for your own mistakes while imagining yourself innocent injures the soul rather than honoring it. Chasing pleasure against the lawgiver's guidance fills the soul with corruption and regret. Surrendering before necessary hardships makes it dishonorable. Even clinging to life at all costs dishonors the soul -- for when it fears death as pure evil, we should challenge that belief rather than yield to it. The afterlife may prove the greatest blessing of all.
summary: The Athenian catalogs ways people dishonor their souls while believing the opposite: blaming others for one's own faults, pursuing illicit pleasures, abandoning approved hardships, and clinging to life at any cost out of fear of death.
distilled: Blaming others for your faults, fleeing hardship, chasing pleasure, and fearing death at all costs are forms of soul-injury disguised as self-preservation.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The theme of self-deception about one's own virtue connects to Socrates' insistence in the Apology (38a) that the unexamined life is not worth living. The idea that yielding to fear of death dishonors the soul parallels Phaedo 67d-68b, where the philosopher practices for death. The legislator's 'approved pains' -- endurance of hardship -- echo the Spartan educational model (agoge) familiar to Megillus, and the Republic's guardian training (III.386a-392c). The suggestion that the afterlife may be good rather than evil anticipates the eschatological myths of the Phaedo, Gorgias, and Republic X.

narrative:
"Again, when any one prefers beauty to virtue, what is this but the real and utter dishonour of the soul? For such a preference implies that the body is more honourable than the soul; and this is false, for there is nothing of earthly birth which is more honourable than the heavenly, and he who thinks otherwise of the soul has no idea how greatly he undervalues this wonderful possession; nor, again, when a person is willing, or not unwilling, to acquire dishonest gains, does he then honour his soul with gifts -- far otherwise; he sells her glory and honour for a small piece of gold; but all the gold which is under or upon the earth is not enough to give in exchange for virtue.

"In a word, I may say that he who does not estimate the base and evil, the good and noble, according to the standard of the legislator, and abstain in every possible way from the one and practise the other to the utmost of his power, does not know that in all these respects he is most foully and disgracefully abusing his soul, which is the divinest part of man; for no one, as I may say, ever considers that which is declared to be the greatest penalty of evil-doing -- namely, to grow into the likeness of bad men, and growing like them to fly from the conversation of the good, and be cut off from them, and cleave to and follow after the company of the bad.

"And he who is joined to them must do and suffer what such men by nature do and say to one another -- a suffering which is not justice but retribution; for justice and the just are noble, whereas retribution is the suffering which waits upon injustice; and whether a man escape or endure this, he is miserable -- in the former case, because he is not cured; while in the latter, he perishes in order that the rest of mankind may be saved."
refashioned:
When someone values physical beauty over moral excellence, this is nothing less than a complete dishonoring of the soul. Such a preference treats the body as more worthy than the soul, which is false -- nothing of earthly origin outranks what is divine. Anyone who thinks otherwise has no grasp of how profoundly they are devaluing their most precious possession.

Likewise, when a person willingly acquires dishonest profits, they do not honor their soul with these gains. Quite the opposite -- they sell the soul's glory and dignity for a pittance of gold. Yet all the gold beneath and upon the earth would not suffice as a fair exchange for virtue.

In short, whoever fails to judge good and evil by the lawgiver's standard -- abstaining from what is base and practicing what is noble with all their strength -- does not realize they are committing the most shameful abuse against the most divine part of themselves. No one considers what is truly the greatest punishment for wrongdoing: becoming like wicked people. As you grow to resemble them, you flee the company of the good and attach yourself to the bad. Joined to them, you must endure what such people naturally inflict on one another.

This suffering is not justice but retribution. Justice and what is just are noble things; retribution is merely the consequence that shadows injustice. Whether a person escapes this retribution or endures it, they are wretched either way -- escaping it, they remain uncured; enduring it, they perish so that the rest of humanity may be preserved.
elegant: Preferring beauty over virtue dishonors the soul by treating the body as superior to what is divine. Acquiring dishonest wealth sells the soul's dignity for a pittance -- all the gold on earth cannot purchase virtue. The greatest punishment for wrongdoing goes unrecognized: becoming like the wicked, fleeing good company, and binding yourself to the bad. The suffering that follows is not justice but retribution -- the shadow consequence of injustice. Whether you escape or endure it, you are wretched: escaping means remaining uncured; enduring means perishing so others may be saved.
summary: Preferring physical beauty to virtue and pursuing dishonest gain both degrade the soul. The greatest unrecognized penalty of wrongdoing is moral transformation -- becoming like bad people and losing the company of the good. The resulting suffering is retribution, not justice, and leaves the wrongdoer wretched regardless of outcome.
distilled: The true penalty of wrongdoing is not external punishment but internal transformation -- you become like the wicked, lose the company of the good, and no amount of gold can buy back virtue.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The claim that all earth's gold cannot purchase virtue echoes Apology 30b and Euthydemus 281d-e. The idea that wrongdoers become like the wicked through moral contagion parallels Republic X.611b-612a (the soul encrusted by association with the body) and Theaetetus 176a-177a (assimilation to God vs. assimilation to evil). The distinction between justice as noble and retribution as mere consequence is a refinement of the punishment theory in Gorgias 472d-479e, where Socrates argues punishment is beneficial when it cures. The notion that the incurable perish for the salvation of others anticipates the discussion of capital punishment later in the Laws (IX.862d-863a).

narrative:
"Speaking generally, our glory is to follow the better and improve the inferior, which is susceptible of improvement, as far as this is possible. And of all human possessions, the soul is by nature most inclined to avoid the evil, and track out and find the chief good; which when a man has found, he should take up his abode with it during the remainder of his life. Wherefore the soul also is second [or next to God] in honour; and third, as every one will perceive, comes the honour of the body in natural order.

"Having determined this, we have next to consider that there is a natural honour of the body, and that of honours some are true and some are counterfeit. To decide which are which is the business of the legislator; and he, I suspect, would intimate that they are as follows: Honour is not to be given to the fair body, or to the strong or the swift or the tall, or to the healthy body (although many may think otherwise), any more than to their opposites; but the mean states of all these habits are by far the safest and most moderate; for the one extreme makes the soul braggart and insolent, and the other, illiberal and base; and money, and property, and distinction all go to the same tune.

"The excess of any of these things is apt to be a source of hatreds and divisions among states and individuals; and the defect of them is commonly a cause of slavery. And, therefore, I would not have any one fond of heaping up riches for the sake of his children, in order that he may leave them as rich as possible. For the possession of great wealth is of no use, either to them or to the state. The condition of youth which is free from flattery, and at the same time not in need of the necessaries of life, is the best and most harmonious of all, being in accord and agreement with our nature, and making life to be most entirely free from sorrow. Let parents, then, bequeath to their children not a heap of riches, but the spirit of reverence. We, indeed, fancy that they will inherit reverence from us, if we rebuke them when they show a want of reverence."
refashioned:
As a general principle, our highest purpose is to follow what is better and improve what is lesser, insofar as improvement is possible. Of all human possessions, the soul is by nature most inclined to shun evil and seek out the highest good. Once a person discovers that good, they should dwell with it for the rest of their life. This is why the soul ranks second in honor after the gods, and the body comes third, as anyone can see.

Having established this hierarchy, we must recognize that there is a natural honor belonging to the body, but that some honors are genuine and others are counterfeit. Distinguishing between them is the lawgiver's task. I believe the lawgiver would say this: honor should not be given to the beautiful body, nor to the strong, the swift, the tall, or the healthy -- any more than to their opposites. The moderate middle ground in all these qualities is by far the safest and soundest. One extreme breeds arrogance and insolence in the soul; the other breeds servility and meanness. The same pattern holds for money, property, and social distinction.

Excess in any of these tends to generate hatred and division among nations and individuals, while deficiency commonly leads to enslavement. Therefore no one should be eager to pile up wealth for their children, hoping to leave them as rich as possible. Great wealth serves neither the children nor the state. The best and most harmonious condition for the young is to be free from flattery yet supplied with life's necessities -- this accords with our nature and produces a life most free from suffering.

Let parents bequeath to their children not heaps of money, but the spirit of reverence. We imagine they will inherit reverence from us if we scold them when they fail to show it.
elegant: Our highest purpose is to follow the better and improve the lesser. The soul, naturally inclined to seek the good and flee evil, ranks second in honor after the gods; the body comes third. But bodily honors can be genuine or counterfeit. Neither beauty, strength, speed, nor health deserve special honor -- nor do their opposites. Moderation is safest: one extreme breeds arrogance, the other servility. The same applies to wealth and status. Excess generates hatred and division; deficiency leads to enslavement. Parents should not hoard riches for their children -- great wealth serves neither child nor state. The best condition for youth is freedom from both flattery and want. Bequeath reverence, not riches.
summary: The Athenian establishes a three-tier hierarchy of honor: gods first, soul second, body third. Moderate physical qualities and moderate wealth are preferable to extremes, which breed either arrogance or servility. Parents should leave their children reverence rather than riches.
distilled: Honor gods first, soul second, body third -- and bequeath your children reverence rather than wealth, since extremes of fortune breed arrogance or servility.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The tripartite hierarchy (gods, soul, body) structures the entire ethical preamble of Book V. The doctrine of the mean -- that moderate states are safest -- anticipates Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics II.6 but is already present in Plato's Statesman (284a-e). The warning against excessive wealth for children echoes Republic III.416d-417b (guardians must not possess gold/silver) and anticipates the property limits established later in Laws V (744d-745b). The idea that wealth breeds faction echoes Republic VIII.550d-551b on the oligarchic city. The claim that parents wrongly think scolding produces reverence sets up the next passage on elder example.

narrative:
"But this quality is not really imparted to them by the present style of admonition, which only tells them that the young ought always to be reverential. A sensible legislator will rather exhort the elders to reverence the younger, and above all to take heed that no young man sees or hears one of themselves doing or saying anything disgraceful; for where old men have no shame, there young men will most certainly be devoid of reverence. The best way of training the young is to train yourself at the same time; not to admonish them, but to be always carrying out your own admonitions in practice.

"He who honours his kindred, and reveres those who share in the same Gods and are of the same blood and family, may fairly expect that the Gods who preside over generation will be propitious to him, and will quicken his seed. And he who deems the services which his friends and acquaintances do for him, greater and more important than they themselves deem them, and his own favours to them less than theirs to him, will have their good-will in the intercourse of life.

"And surely in his relations to the state and his fellow citizens, he is by far the best, who rather than the Olympic or any other victory of peace or war, desires to win the palm of obedience to the laws of his country, and who, of all mankind, is the person reputed to have obeyed them best through life.

"In his relations to strangers, a man should consider that a contract is a most holy thing, and that all concerns and wrongs of strangers are more directly dependent on the protection of God, than wrongs done to citizens; for the stranger, having no kindred and friends, is more to be pitied by Gods and men."
refashioned:
But reverence is not truly instilled by the usual kind of lecturing, which simply tells young people they should always be respectful. A wise lawgiver would instead urge the elders to show reverence toward the young, and above all to ensure that no young person witnesses them doing or saying anything shameful. Where old people have no sense of shame, young people will inevitably lack reverence. The most effective way to educate the young is to educate yourself alongside them -- not by preaching, but by consistently living out your own principles.

Whoever honors their family and shows respect for those who share the same gods, bloodline, and household can reasonably hope that the gods who govern birth and generation will look upon them with favor. And whoever regards the favors others do for them as greater than those others consider them, while regarding their own favors to others as smaller, will enjoy lasting goodwill in all their relationships.

In one's relationship to the state and fellow citizens, the finest person is not the one who seeks an Olympic victory or any other prize of peace or war, but the one who aspires to the distinction of lifelong obedience to the laws -- the person renowned above all others for having best obeyed them throughout their life.

As for dealings with foreigners, one should regard every contract as sacred. The wrongs and concerns of strangers depend more directly on divine protection than those of citizens, because the stranger, having no family or friends to advocate for them, is more deserving of compassion from both gods and mortals.
elegant: Reverence is not taught by lecturing the young but by the elders' example. Where old people feel no shame, young people will show no respect. The best education is practicing what you preach. Honor your family and the gods will favor you. Value others' kindnesses above your own and you will keep their goodwill. The finest citizen is not the Olympic champion but the one renowned for lifelong obedience to the laws. And contracts with strangers are sacred -- foreigners, lacking family to protect them, depend directly on divine justice and deserve special compassion.
summary: Reverence is taught by example, not by lectures -- elders must model the behavior they demand. The best citizen prizes lawful obedience above any competitive victory. Contracts with strangers are especially sacred because foreigners lack the protective networks of citizens.
distilled: The young learn reverence not from what elders say but from what they do, and the greatest civic achievement is not competitive glory but lifelong obedience to just laws.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The principle that education works through example rather than admonition echoes Republic III.395c-d (guardians shaped by what they imitate) and the Protagoras (325c-326e) on moral education. The elevation of law-abidingness over Olympic victory is striking -- Olympic victors received enormous honor in Greek culture (cf. Pindar's odes). This inverts conventional Greek values, as Xenophanes fragment 2 similarly argues. The sanctity of obligations to strangers (xenoi) reflects Zeus Xenios, Zeus as protector of guests and strangers -- a foundational principle of Greek religion referenced in the Odyssey. This passage transitions from private ethics to public and international obligations.

narrative:
"Wherefore, also, he who is most able to avenge him is most zealous in his cause; and he who is most able is the genius and the god of the stranger, who follow in the train of Zeus, the god of strangers. And for this reason, he who has a spark of caution in him, will do his best to pass through life without sinning against the stranger.

"And of offences committed, whether against strangers or fellow-countrymen, that against suppliants is the greatest. For the god who witnessed to the agreement made with the suppliant, becomes in a special manner the guardian of the sufferer; and he will certainly not suffer unavenged."
refashioned:
For this reason, the being most capable of avenging wrongs against the stranger is also the most zealous in the stranger's cause -- and that being is the divine guardian spirit that accompanies each stranger, following in the train of Zeus, the god of hospitality and foreign guests. Anyone with even a trace of prudence will therefore strive to pass through life without committing any offense against a stranger.

Of all offenses, whether against strangers or fellow citizens, the greatest is the wrong done to suppliants. The god who witnessed the compact made with the suppliant becomes their particular guardian, and their suffering will certainly not go unavenged.
elegant: The guardian spirit attending each stranger zealously avenges wrongs against foreigners. Any prudent person avoids offending strangers. Of all wrongs, offenses against suppliants are gravest -- the witnessing god becomes their protector, and their suffering will not go unavenged.
summary: The divine spirits attending strangers, under Zeus the god of hospitality, zealously avenge wrongs committed against foreigners. The gravest of all offenses is wronging a suppliant, whose divine witness becomes their special guardian.
distilled: Offenses against strangers and suppliants are the most divinely punished because Zeus and the attending guardian spirits zealously protect those without human advocates.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. Zeus Xenios (Zeus of strangers/guests) was one of the most important cult titles of Zeus in Greek religion. The 'genius' (daimon) of the stranger refers to the personal divine attendant -- cf. Phaedo 107d-108c and Republic X.617d-620d on daimones assigned to souls. Supplication (hikesia) was a sacred institution in Greek culture; violating it was considered among the most impious acts. This brief passage caps the discussion of obligations to strangers and provides theological enforcement of the social norms discussed in 5.5. Cross-reference Euthyphro 12e on piety as the part of justice concerning the gods.

narrative: "Thus we have fairly described the manner in which a man is to act about his parents, and himself, and his own affairs; and in relation to the state, and his friends, and kindred, both in what concerns his own countrymen, and in what concerns the stranger. We will now consider what manner of man he must be who would best pass through life in respect of those other things which are not matters of law, but of praise and blame only; in which praise and blame educate a man, and make him more tractable and amenable to the laws which are about to be imposed."
refashioned: We have now adequately described how a person should conduct themselves regarding their parents, their own soul, their personal affairs, their obligations to the state, friends, family, fellow citizens, and foreigners. Let us now turn to consider what kind of person would best navigate those areas of life that fall outside the domain of law proper -- matters governed not by legal compulsion but by praise and blame alone. It is through such praise and blame that character is shaped, making a person more willing and receptive to the laws that are about to be established.
elegant: Having covered one's duties to parents, self, state, kin, friends, citizens, and strangers, we now turn to matters beyond law's reach -- areas governed only by praise and blame. These shape character and make people receptive to the laws about to be imposed.
summary: The Athenian summarizes the ethical duties already covered and transitions to a new topic: the informal moral education achieved through praise and blame, which prepares citizens to accept formal laws.
distilled: Beyond what law can compel, praise and blame shape character and prepare citizens to willingly accept legal obligations.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This transitional passage reveals the Laws' distinctive approach to legislation -- the lengthy 'preambles' (prooimia) that precede actual legal provisions. The Athenian introduced this method in Book IV (722d-723d), arguing that laws should persuade, not merely command. The distinction between matters of law and matters of praise/blame anticipates Aristotle's discussion of the limits of legislation in Nicomachean Ethics X.9 (1179b-1180a). This also reflects the Greek concept of aidos (shame/reverence) as a social enforcement mechanism alongside written law.

narrative:
"Truth is the beginning of every good thing, both to Gods and men; and he who would be blessed and happy, should be from the first a partaker of the truth, that he may live a true man as long as possible, for then he can be trusted; but he is not to be trusted who loves voluntary falsehood, and he who loves involuntary falsehood is a fool. Neither condition is enviable, for the untrustworthy and ignorant has no friend, and as time advances he becomes known, and lays up in store for himself isolation in crabbed age when life is on the wane: so that, whether his children or friends are alive or not, he is equally solitary.

"Worthy of honour is he who does no injustice, and of more than twofold honour, if he not only does no injustice himself, but hinders others from doing any; the first may count as one man, the second is worth many men, because he informs the rulers of the injustice of others. And yet more highly to be esteemed is he who co-operates with the rulers in correcting the citizens as far as he can -- he shall be proclaimed the great and perfect citizen, and bear away the palm of virtue.

"The same praise may be given about temperance and wisdom, and all other goods which may be imparted to others, as well as acquired by a man for himself; he who imparts them shall be honoured as the man of men, and he who is willing, yet is not able, may be allowed the second place; but he who is jealous and will not, if he can help, allow others to partake in a friendly way of any good, is deserving of blame: the good, however, which he has, is not to be undervalued by us because it is possessed by him, but must be acquired by us also to the utmost of our power. Let every man, then, freely strive for the prize of virtue, and let there be no envy."
refashioned:
Truth is the foundation of every good thing, for gods and mortals alike. Whoever seeks to be blessed and happy should embrace truth from the very beginning, so they may live authentically for as long as possible and thus be worthy of trust. The person who loves deliberate falsehood cannot be trusted, and the person who loves unintentional falsehood is simply a fool. Neither condition is desirable, for the untrustworthy and the ignorant alike end up friendless. As time passes, their true nature becomes known, and they store up for themselves a bitter isolation in old age -- equally alone whether their children and friends are living or not.

The person who commits no injustice deserves honor. But the person who not only avoids injustice but actively prevents others from committing it deserves more than double that honor. The first counts as one good person; the second is worth many, because they alert the authorities to wrongdoing. Even more highly esteemed is the person who cooperates with rulers in reforming citizens as far as they are able -- such a person should be proclaimed the great and complete citizen, carrying off the supreme prize of virtue.

The same principle applies to temperance, wisdom, and every other good that can be both possessed and shared. Whoever transmits these goods to others should be honored as the finest of all people. Whoever is willing but unable to share them may claim second place. But whoever jealously hoards what is good and refuses to let others benefit deserves censure. Still, we should not devalue the good simply because an unworthy person possesses it -- we must strive to acquire it ourselves with all our strength. Let everyone compete freely for the prize of virtue, and let there be no envy.
elegant: Truth is the foundation of all good for gods and mortals. The voluntary liar cannot be trusted; the involuntary one is a fool. Both end in friendless isolation. Honor belongs to whoever commits no injustice, but far greater honor to whoever prevents injustice in others and cooperates with rulers in reforming citizens -- this person is the complete citizen. The same holds for every virtue: those who share wisdom and temperance are the finest people; those who hoard them deserve blame. But never devalue a good because an unworthy person holds it -- pursue virtue yourself. Compete freely, and let there be no envy.
summary: Truth is the foundation of all goodness. The liar and the fool both end in isolation. Active prevention of injustice deserves far more honor than merely abstaining from it, and sharing one's virtues with others is the mark of the greatest citizen.
distilled: The highest citizen is not merely the one who avoids wrong but the one who actively prevents injustice in others and freely shares virtue without envy.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The opening claim that truth is the beginning of every good recalls Socrates' commitment to truth in the Apology (18a) and the Allegory of the Cave in Republic VII (514a-521b), where truth is the highest form of illumination. The hierarchy of citizens -- passive non-offender, active preventer, cooperative reformer -- builds on Republic I.331c-335e (justice as more than non-harm). The condemnation of jealous hoarding of virtue inverts the zero-sum thinking about goods discussed in Republic II.358e-359b (Glaucon's challenge). The 'voluntary vs. involuntary falsehood' distinction echoes Hippias Minor 365-376 on whether voluntary wrongdoing is better than involuntary.

narrative:
"For the unenvious nature increases the greatness of states -- he himself contends in the race, blasting the fair fame of no man; but the envious, who thinks that he ought to get the better by defaming others, is less energetic himself in the pursuit of true virtue, and reduces his rivals to despair by his unjust slanders of them. And so he makes the whole city to enter the arena untrained in the practice of virtue, and diminishes her glory as far as in him lies.

"Now every man should be valiant, but he should also be gentle. From the cruel, or hardly curable, or altogether incurable acts of injustice done to him by others, a man can only escape by fighting and defending himself and conquering, and by never ceasing to punish them; and no man who is not of a noble spirit is able to accomplish this.

"As to the actions of those who do evil, but whose evil is curable, in the first place, let us remember that the unjust man is not unjust of his own free will. For no man of his own free will would choose to possess the greatest of evils, and least of all in the most honourable part of himself. And the soul, as we said, is of a truth deemed by all men the most honourable. In the soul, then, which is the most honourable part of him, no one, if he could help, would admit, or allow to continue the greatest of evils.

"The unrighteous and vicious are always to be pitied in any case; and one can afford to forgive as well as pity him who is curable, and refrain and calm one's anger, not getting into a passion, like a woman, and nursing ill-feeling. But upon him who is incapable of reformation and wholly evil, the vials of our wrath should be poured out; wherefore I say that good men ought, when occasion demands, to be both gentle and passionate."
refashioned:
The person free from envy increases the greatness of their state. They compete honestly, without tarnishing anyone else's reputation. But the envious person, who believes they must advance by tearing others down, puts less energy into genuine virtue and drives their competitors to despair through unfair slander. In this way, the envious person sends the whole city into the arena of life untrained in virtue, diminishing its collective excellence as much as they possibly can.

Every person should be courageous, but they should also be gentle. When facing cruel, barely curable, or altogether incurable acts of injustice from others, the only way to protect oneself is by fighting back, defending oneself, and prevailing -- never relenting in holding wrongdoers accountable. No one lacking noble spirit can accomplish this.

But regarding those whose wrongdoing is curable, we must first remember a crucial principle: the unjust person is not unjust by their own free choice. No one would voluntarily possess the greatest of evils, least of all in the most honorable part of themselves. The soul, as we have established, is universally recognized as a person's most honorable part. No one would willingly admit or maintain the greatest evil in their soul if they could prevent it.

The unrighteous and vicious are always deserving of pity. When someone's vice is curable, we can afford to show forgiveness as well as compassion -- restraining and calming our anger rather than indulging in bitter resentment. But against those who are incapable of reform and thoroughly corrupt, righteous anger should be unleashed in full force. Therefore, good people must be prepared, as the occasion demands, to be both gentle and fierce.
elegant: The unenvious person competes honestly, elevating the whole city; the envious person slanders rivals, undermining everyone's pursuit of virtue. Every person should be both courageous and gentle. Against incurable injustice, one must fight and never relent. But for curable wrongdoing, remember: no one is unjust willingly. No one would voluntarily harbor the greatest evil in their soul, their most honored possession. The vicious deserve pity; the reformable deserve forgiveness and restraint. Only against the irredeemably corrupt should righteous anger be unleashed. The good must know when to be gentle and when to be fierce.
summary: Envy undermines a whole city's virtue, while honest competition elevates it. People should be both courageous and gentle: pitifully forgiving toward curable wrongdoers -- since no one is voluntarily unjust -- but fiercely wrathful against the irredeemably corrupt.
distilled: No one is willingly unjust, so the curable wrongdoer deserves pity and forgiveness, but the irredeemably corrupt must face the full force of righteous anger.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The claim that no one is unjust willingly is one of Plato's most famous and controversial doctrines, appearing in Protagoras 345d-e, Gorgias 509e, Republic III.413a, and Timaeus 86d-e. It grounds the rehabilitative theory of punishment central to the Laws. The distinction between curable and incurable wrongdoers echoes Gorgias 525b-526d, where the incurably wicked serve as eternal examples in Tartarus. The gendered remark about anger 'like a woman' reflects pervasive Greek cultural assumptions (cf. Republic V.455d-e, which paradoxically argues for women's equality). The dual requirement of gentleness and fierceness for the good person parallels Republic II.375a-376c on the guardian nature being both spirited and philosophical.

narrative:
"Of all evils the greatest is one which in the souls of most men is innate, and which a man is always excusing in himself and never correcting; I mean, what is expressed in the saying that 'Every man by nature is and ought to be his own friend.' Whereas the excessive love of self is in reality the source to each man of all offences; for the lover is blinded about the beloved, so that he judges wrongly of the just, the good, and the honourable, and thinks that he ought always to prefer himself to the truth.

"But he who would be a great man ought to regard, not himself or his interests, but what is just, whether the just act be his own or that of another. Through a similar error men are induced to fancy that their own ignorance is wisdom, and thus we who may be truly said to know nothing, think that we know all things; and because we will not let others act for us in what we do not know, we are compelled to act amiss ourselves.

"Wherefore let every man avoid excess of self-love, and condescend to follow a better man than himself, not allowing any false shame to stand in the way. There are also minor precepts which are often repeated, and are quite as useful; a man should recollect them and remind himself of them."
refashioned:
The greatest of all evils is one that is innate in most human souls -- one that a person constantly excuses in themselves and never corrects. I am speaking of what the old saying captures: that every person by nature is and ought to be their own best friend. In reality, this excessive self-love is the root of all wrongdoing. Just as a lover is blind to the faults of the beloved, the self-lover judges poorly in matters of justice, goodness, and honor, believing they should always put themselves ahead of the truth.

But whoever aspires to true greatness must look beyond themselves and their personal interests toward what is just -- regardless of whether the just action is their own or someone else's. Through a similar error of self-regard, people mistake their own ignorance for wisdom. We who truly know nothing convince ourselves that we know everything, and because we refuse to let others handle what we do not understand, we are forced into constant blundering.

Therefore, let everyone guard against excessive self-love and be willing to follow someone wiser than themselves, setting aside false pride. There are also smaller principles, often repeated, that are equally valuable -- a person should keep them in mind and regularly remind themselves of them.
elegant: The greatest innate evil in the human soul is excessive self-love -- the root of all wrongdoing. Like any lover blinded about the beloved, the self-lover misjudges justice, goodness, and honor, always placing themselves above truth. True greatness requires caring about what is just, not about one's own advantage. The same self-regard makes us mistake our ignorance for wisdom: knowing nothing, we think we know everything, and refuse to defer to those who actually understand. Guard against excessive self-love, follow those wiser than yourself, and set aside false pride.
summary: Excessive self-love is the greatest innate evil, blinding people to justice and truth just as romantic love blinds lovers. It also produces the delusion that one's own ignorance is wisdom. The remedy is humility -- willingness to follow those who are wiser.
distilled: Excessive self-love is the root of all wrongdoing because it blinds us to truth, makes us mistake ignorance for wisdom, and prevents us from deferring to those who know better.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The diagnosis of self-love (philautia) as the greatest evil is distinctive to the Laws and represents Plato's late moral psychology. It connects to the Socratic paradox that no one errs willingly (5.9) -- self-love is the mechanism of self-deception that enables involuntary wrongdoing. The image of the lover blinded about the beloved echoes Phaedrus 238e-241d on erotic madness. The claim that we mistake ignorance for wisdom is the core insight of the Apology (21b-23b), where Socrates discovers that those who think themselves wise are most ignorant. Cf. also Alcibiades I (133c) on the danger of self-ignorance. The injunction to follow a better person recalls Socrates' method of following the argument wherever it leads (Republic 394d).

narrative:
"For when a stream is flowing out, there should be water flowing in too; and recollection flows in while wisdom is departing. Therefore I say that a man should refrain from excess either of laughter or tears, and should exhort his neighbour to do the same; he should veil his immoderate sorrow or joy, and seek to behave with propriety, whether the genius of his good fortune remains with him, or whether at the crisis of his fate, when he seems to be mounting high and steep places, the Gods oppose him in some of his enterprises.

"Still he may ever hope, in the case of good men, that whatever afflictions are to befall them in the future God will lessen, and that present evils he will change for the better; and as to the goods which are the opposite of these evils, he will not doubt that they will be added to them, and that they will be fortunate. Such should be men's hopes, and such should be the exhortations with which they admonish one another, never losing an opportunity, but on every occasion distinctly reminding themselves and others of all these things, both in jest and earnest."
refashioned:
When a stream is flowing out, there ought to be water flowing in as well. In the same way, recollection should flow in as wisdom naturally fades. For this reason, a person should avoid excessive laughter or tears and encourage their neighbors to do likewise. They should restrain immoderate displays of sorrow or joy and conduct themselves with composure -- whether their guardian spirit of good fortune remains with them, or whether the gods oppose some of their ventures at crucial moments when fate seems to be driving them up steep and treacherous heights.

Nevertheless, good people may always maintain the hope that God will lighten whatever afflictions await them in the future and transform their present sufferings for the better. As for the blessings that are the counterpart of these troubles, they should have no doubt that these too will come, bringing good fortune in their wake. Such should be the hopes people carry, and such should be the encouragements they share with one another -- never missing an opportunity, but at every turn reminding themselves and others of these truths, in both lighthearted and serious moments.
elegant: As wisdom naturally fades, recollection must flow in to replace it -- like water replenishing a stream. Therefore restrain excessive laughter and tears alike, and maintain composure whether fortune favors you or the gods oppose your ventures. Good people may always hope that God will lighten future afflictions, transform present evils for the better, and add corresponding blessings. Carry such hopes and share them constantly with one another, in both jest and earnest.
summary: People should maintain emotional composure in both fortune and misfortune, restraining excessive displays of joy or grief. The good may always hope that God will lighten their afflictions and improve their circumstances, and they should constantly remind one another of this.
distilled: Maintain composure in fortune and misfortune alike, and never cease reminding yourself and others that the gods lighten the afflictions of the good.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The stream metaphor for recollection replacing departing wisdom connects to the theory of anamnesis (recollection) in Meno 81c-86c and Phaedo 72e-77a, though here applied practically rather than metaphysically. The counsel of emotional moderation -- neither excessive joy nor grief -- echoes the Stoic ideal of apatheia that Plato's student Polemo would develop, and prefigures Aristotle's doctrine of the mean in emotional responses (NE II.6). The 'genius' (daimon) of good fortune recalls the personal daimon of Socrates (Apology 31d, Phaedrus 242b-c). The instruction to maintain hope in divine providence represents the Laws' distinctive theological optimism, contrasted with the more philosophical consolation of the Phaedo.

narrative:
"Enough has now been said of divine matters, both as touching the practices which men ought to follow, and as to the sort of persons who they ought severally to be. But of human things we have not as yet spoken, and we must; for to men we are discoursing and not to Gods.

"Pleasures and pains and desires are a part of human nature, and on them every mortal being must of necessity hang and depend with the most eager interest. And therefore we must praise the noblest life, not only as the fairest in appearance, but as being one which, if a man will only taste, and not, while still in his youth, desert for another, he will find to surpass also in the very thing which we all of us desire -- I mean in having a greater amount of pleasure and less of pain during the whole of life. And this will be plain, if a man has a true taste of them, as will be quickly and clearly seen."
refashioned:
We have now said enough about divine matters -- both the practices people should follow and the kind of persons they ought to be. But we have not yet addressed human concerns, and we must, since our audience is mortal, not divine.

Pleasures, pains, and desires are fundamental to human nature. Every mortal being necessarily depends on them with the keenest interest. Therefore, we must commend the noblest life not merely as the most admirable in appearance, but as one that -- if a person will only experience it genuinely and not abandon it in youth for something else -- actually surpasses all others in the very thing everyone desires most: a greater share of pleasure and a lesser share of pain across the whole course of life. This will become evident to anyone who truly samples these lives, as will be quickly and clearly demonstrated.
elegant: Having addressed divine matters, we must now turn to human ones -- our audience is mortal, after all. Pleasures, pains, and desires are inescapable parts of human nature. The noblest life deserves praise not only for its moral beauty but because it actually delivers what everyone wants most: more pleasure and less pain over a lifetime. Anyone who genuinely tastes this life without abandoning it in youth will discover this to be true.
summary: Transitioning from divine to human concerns, the Athenian argues that the virtuous life deserves praise not only for its moral excellence but because it genuinely produces more pleasure and less pain over a lifetime than any alternative.
distilled: The noblest life is not only the most admirable but also the most genuinely pleasurable -- delivering more joy and less suffering across an entire lifetime.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This marks a major structural transition in the preamble from divine/ethical to human/hedonistic considerations. The argument that virtue is also the most pleasant life is a cornerstone of the Laws and echoes the Republic's demonstration in Book IX (580d-588a) that the philosopher's pleasures are the truest and most intense. The strategy of appealing to pleasure to motivate virtue reflects the Laws' more pragmatic orientation compared to the Republic's more purely philosophical arguments. Cf. Protagoras 351b-358d, where Socrates controversially argues that virtue and pleasure may be identified. The emphasis on 'tasting' (experience) rather than mere argument suggests an empirical rather than purely dialectical approach.

narrative:
"But what is a true taste? That we have to learn from the argument -- the point being what is according to nature, and what is not according to nature. One life must be compared with another, the more pleasurable with the more painful, after this manner: We desire to have pleasure, but we neither desire nor choose pain; and the neutral state we are ready to take in exchange, not for pleasure but for pain; and we also wish for less pain and greater pleasure, but less pleasure and greater pain we do not wish for; and an equal balance of either we cannot venture to assert that we should desire.

"And all these differ or do not differ severally in number and magnitude and intensity and equality, and in the opposites of these when regarded as objects of choice, in relation to desire. And such being the necessary order of things, we wish for that life in which there are many great and intense elements of pleasure and pain, and in which the pleasures are in excess, and do not wish for that in which the opposites exceed; nor, again, do we wish for that in which the elements of either are small and few and feeble, and the pains exceed.

"And when, as I said before, there is a balance of pleasure and pain in life, this is to be regarded by us as the balanced life; while other lives are preferred by us because they exceed in what we like, or are rejected by us because they exceed in what we dislike. All the lives of men may be regarded by us as bound up in these, and we must also consider what sort of lives we by nature desire. And if we wish for any others, I say that we desire them only through some ignorance and inexperience of the lives which actually exist."
refashioned:
But what constitutes a genuine experience of these lives? We must determine this through argument, specifically by distinguishing what accords with nature from what does not. Lives must be compared with one another -- the more pleasurable with the more painful -- using the following framework.

We desire pleasure but neither desire nor choose pain. We would accept a neutral state as a substitute for pain but not for pleasure. We wish for less pain and more pleasure, but we do not wish for less pleasure and more pain. Whether we would choose an equal balance of the two is uncertain.

All these states differ in number, magnitude, intensity, and proportion, and their desirability depends on these variables. Given this natural ordering, we desire a life containing many great and intense experiences of both pleasure and pain -- provided the pleasures predominate. We reject any life where pains predominate. Nor do we desire a life where both pleasures and pains are few, small, and weak, yet the pains still outweigh the pleasures.

When pleasure and pain are in equal balance, we may call this the balanced life. We prefer lives that exceed in what we enjoy and reject those that exceed in what we find painful. All human lives fall within these categories, and we must consider which ones we naturally desire. If we seem to desire any life outside these categories, it is only through ignorance and inexperience of the lives that actually exist.
elegant: What is a genuine experience of different lives? We must use argument to determine what accords with nature. We desire pleasure, not pain; we accept neutrality as a substitute for pain but not for pleasure. The desirability of any life depends on the number, magnitude, intensity, and balance of its pleasures and pains. We want the life where pleasures predominate, reject the one where pains predominate, and are uncertain about perfect balance. All human lives fall within this framework. If we seem to want something outside it, that desire stems from ignorance of the lives that actually exist.
summary: The Athenian develops a systematic framework for evaluating lives based on the balance of pleasure and pain. We naturally desire the life where pleasures predominate in number, intensity, and magnitude. Any desire for a life outside this framework stems from ignorance.
distilled: All human lives are evaluated by the balance of their pleasures and pains -- we naturally choose the life where pleasures predominate, and any contrary desire reflects ignorance.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage develops a proto-utilitarian calculus of pleasure and pain that anticipates Epicurus and Bentham. It extends the hedonistic argument begun in the Protagoras (356a-357e), where Socrates proposes an 'art of measurement' for pleasures and pains. The emphasis on nature as the standard connects to Philebus 31a-55c, Plato's most sustained analysis of pleasure. The claim that mistaken life-choices arise from 'ignorance and inexperience' reinforces the Socratic intellectualism of 5.9-5.10 (wrongdoing from ignorance). The analytical categories (number, magnitude, intensity, equality) prefigure Aristotle's treatment of pleasure in NE VII.11-14 and X.1-5.

narrative:
"Now, what lives are they, and how many in which, having searched out and beheld the objects of will and desire and their opposites, and making of them a law, choosing, I say, the dear and the pleasant and the best and noblest, a man may live in the happiest way possible? Let us say that the temperate life is one kind of life, and the rational another, and the courageous another, and the healthful another; and to these four let us oppose four other lives -- the foolish, the cowardly, the intemperate, the diseased.

"He who knows the temperate life will describe it as in all things gentle, having gentle pains and gentle pleasures, and placid desires and loves not insane; whereas the intemperate life is impetuous in all things, and has violent pains and pleasures, and vehement and stinging desires, and loves utterly insane; and in the temperate life the pleasures exceed the pains, but in the intemperate life the pains exceed the pleasures in greatness and number and frequency. Hence one of the two lives is naturally and necessarily more pleasant and the other more painful, and he who would live pleasantly cannot possibly choose to live intemperately."
refashioned:
What kinds of lives exist in which a person, having examined the objects of will and desire alongside their opposites and established a principle of choice -- selecting what is dear, pleasant, best, and noblest -- can live as happily as possible?

Let us identify four types of virtuous life: the temperate, the rational, the courageous, and the healthy. Against these we set four opposing lives: the foolish, the cowardly, the intemperate, and the diseased.

Anyone who understands the temperate life will describe it as gentle in all respects -- having mild pains and mild pleasures, calm desires, and loves that are sane. The intemperate life, by contrast, is violent in every way: its pains and pleasures are extreme, its desires are fierce and stinging, its loves are utterly deranged. In the temperate life, pleasures exceed pains; in the intemperate life, pains surpass pleasures in magnitude, frequency, and number.

Consequently, one of these two lives is naturally and necessarily more pleasant, the other more painful. Whoever truly wishes to live pleasantly cannot possibly choose the intemperate life.
elegant: Four virtuous lives exist -- temperate, rational, courageous, and healthy -- opposed by four vicious ones: foolish, cowardly, intemperate, and diseased. The temperate life is gentle throughout: mild pleasures, mild pains, calm desires, sane loves. The intemperate life is violent: extreme pleasures and pains, fierce desires, deranged loves. Crucially, in the temperate life pleasures exceed pains, while in the intemperate life the reverse is true. Therefore, whoever genuinely wants to live pleasantly cannot choose intemperance.
summary: The Athenian identifies four virtuous lives (temperate, rational, courageous, healthy) and their vicious opposites. The temperate life, with its gentle pleasures and calm desires, naturally produces more pleasure than pain, while the intemperate life -- despite its violent pleasures -- produces more pain overall.
distilled: The temperate life, with its gentle pleasures and calm desires, is naturally more pleasant than the intemperate life, whose violent pleasures are outweighed by even greater pains.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The four virtuous lives correspond roughly to the four cardinal virtues (temperance, wisdom, courage, and bodily health as a stand-in for justice's social function). This schema simplifies the Republic's more complex treatment. The characterization of intemperate desires as 'insane loves' connects to Republic IX.571b-576b on the tyrannical soul driven by lawless eros. The argument that temperance yields more net pleasure than intemperance is central to the Laws' strategy of making virtue attractive on hedonistic grounds -- cf. Philebus 63d-66d, where the 'mixed life' of pleasure and intelligence is ranked. The pairing of opposites (temperate/intemperate, wise/foolish, etc.) anticipates the method of division in the Sophist and Statesman.

narrative:
"And if this is true, the inference clearly is that no man is voluntarily intemperate; but that the whole multitude of men lack temperance in their lives, either from ignorance, or from want of self-control, or both. And the same holds of the diseased and healthy life; they both have pleasures and pains, but in health the pleasure exceeds the pain, and in sickness the pain exceeds the pleasure.

"Now our intention in choosing the lives is not that the painful should exceed, but the life in which pain is exceeded by pleasure we have determined to be the more pleasant life. And we should say that the temperate life has the elements both of pleasure and pain fewer and smaller and less frequent than the intemperate, and the wise life than the foolish life, and the life of courage than the life of cowardice; one of each pair exceeding in pleasure and the other in pain, the courageous surpassing the cowardly, and the wise exceeding the foolish.

"And so the one class of lives exceeds the other class in pleasure; the temperate and courageous and wise and healthy exceed the cowardly and foolish and intemperate and diseased lives; and generally speaking, that which has any virtue, whether of body or soul, is pleasanter than the vicious life, and far superior in beauty and rectitude and excellence and reputation, and causes him who lives accordingly to be infinitely happier than the opposite."
refashioned:
If this argument holds, the clear conclusion is that no one is voluntarily intemperate. The masses who lack temperance in their lives do so either from ignorance, lack of self-control, or both. The same reasoning applies to the diseased and healthy life: both involve pleasures and pains, but in health pleasure predominates, while in sickness pain does.

Our purpose in evaluating these lives has not been to seek out the life with the greatest pain, but to identify the one where pleasure most exceeds pain. We can now observe that the temperate life has fewer, smaller, and less frequent experiences of both pleasure and pain than the intemperate life; the wise life fewer than the foolish; the courageous life fewer than the cowardly. In each pair, the virtuous life exceeds in pleasure and the vicious one in pain.

Thus the virtuous class of lives -- temperate, courageous, wise, and healthy -- surpasses the vicious class in pleasure. Speaking broadly, any life possessing virtue, whether of body or soul, is more pleasant than its vicious counterpart, and far superior in beauty, moral correctness, excellence, and reputation. The person who lives virtuously is infinitely happier than the one who lives the opposite way.
elegant: No one is voluntarily intemperate -- those who lack temperance do so from ignorance, weak self-control, or both. The temperate life has fewer and milder pleasures and pains than the intemperate, yet its pleasures predominate; the same holds for the wise over the foolish, the courageous over the cowardly, the healthy over the diseased. In every pair, the virtuous life exceeds in net pleasure. Any life possessing virtue is more pleasant, more beautiful, more correct, more excellent, and more reputable than its opposite -- making the virtuous person infinitely happier.
summary: No one chooses intemperance voluntarily -- it results from ignorance or weak self-control. In every comparison between virtuous and vicious lives, the virtuous life contains more net pleasure. Virtue surpasses vice not only in pleasure but also in beauty, moral correctness, excellence, and reputation.
distilled: Virtue wins on every measure -- the temperate, wise, courageous, and healthy lives all produce more net pleasure and greater happiness than their vicious counterparts.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage completes the hedonistic proof of virtue's superiority. The claim that no one is voluntarily intemperate extends the Socratic paradox from 5.9 and 5.14 -- intemperance is reframed as a cognitive and volitional failure rather than a free choice. The explanation via 'ignorance or want of self-control (akrasia)' is notable because Socrates in the Protagoras (352b-358d) famously denies that akrasia exists, reducing all vice to ignorance. Here the Laws acknowledges akrasia as a separate cause alongside ignorance -- a significant concession to common experience that Aristotle would later develop (NE VII.1-10). The conclusion that virtue wins on every dimension (pleasure, beauty, rectitude, reputation) mirrors Republic IX.580b-583a, where the philosophical life is shown to be most pleasant.

narrative:
"Enough of the preamble; and now the laws should follow; or, to speak more correctly, an outline of them. As, then, in the case of a web or any other tissue, the warp and the woof cannot be made of the same materials, but the warp is necessarily superior as being stronger, and having a certain character of firmness, whereas the woof is softer and has a proper degree of elasticity -- in a similar manner those who are to hold great offices in states, should be distinguished truly in each case from those who have been but slenderly proven by education.

"Let us suppose that there are two parts in the constitution of a state -- one the creation of offices, the other the laws which are assigned to them to administer."
refashioned:
The preamble has gone on long enough; now the actual laws should follow -- or, to be more precise, an outline of them.

Consider the analogy of weaving. In any web or fabric, the warp and the woof cannot be made of the same material. The warp must be the stronger thread, possessing firmness and resilience, while the woof is softer and has the proper degree of flexibility. In the same way, those who are to hold high offices in the state must be clearly distinguished from those whose education and testing have been only superficial.

Let us therefore recognize two fundamental components in the constitution of a state: the establishment of public offices, and the laws that those officeholders are charged with administering.
elegant: The preamble is complete; now for the laws themselves -- or rather their outline. Like weaving, where the firm warp threads must differ from the flexible woof, those who hold high office must be distinguished from those only lightly tested by education. A state's constitution has two essential parts: the creation of offices and the laws those offices administer.
summary: The Athenian concludes the ethical preamble and transitions to actual legislation. Using a weaving analogy, he argues that officeholders must be rigorously distinguished from ordinary citizens, and identifies two components of a constitution: the creation of offices and the laws they administer.
distilled: A constitution requires two things -- the creation of offices filled by rigorously tested leaders, and the laws those offices administer -- just as weaving requires both firm warp and flexible woof.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The weaving metaphor is central to the Statesman (279a-283b, 305e-311c), where the art of the statesman is literally compared to the art of weaving -- intertwining the bold/firm (warp) with the gentle/flexible (woof) natures in the citizenry. The transition from preamble to legislation proper marks a structural turning point in Book V. The distinction between offices and laws as the two parts of a constitution anticipates Aristotle's Politics III.6-8 (classification of constitutions by who rules and by what laws). The emphasis on educational testing for officeholders connects to Republic VII.535a-540c on the selection and education of philosopher-rulers.

narrative:
"But, before all this, comes the following consideration: The shepherd or herdsman, or breeder of horses or the like, when he has received his animals will not begin to train them until he has first purified them in a manner which befits a community of animals; he will divide the healthy and unhealthy, and the good breed and the bad breed, and will send away the unhealthy and badly bred to other herds, and tend the rest, reflecting that his labours will be vain and have no effect, either on the souls or bodies of those whom nature and ill nurture have corrupted, and that they will involve in destruction the pure and healthy nature and being of every other animal, if he should neglect to purify them.

"Now the case of other animals is not so important -- they are only worth introducing for the sake of illustration; but what relates to man is of the highest importance; and the legislator should make enquiries, and indicate what is proper for each one in the way of purification and of any other procedure. Take, for example, the purification of a city -- there are many kinds of purification, some easier and others more difficult; and some of them, and the best and most difficult of them, the legislator, if he be also a despot, may be able to effect; but the legislator, who, not being a despot, sets up a new government and laws, even if he attempt the mildest of purgations, may think himself happy if he can complete his work. The best kind of purification is painful, like similar cures in medicine, involving righteous punishment and inflicting death or exile in the last resort."
refashioned:
But before all of this, there is a prior consideration. A shepherd, herdsman, or horse breeder, upon receiving their animals, does not begin training them without first sorting them in a way appropriate to managing a herd. They separate the healthy from the unhealthy, the well-bred from the poorly bred, sending the inferior ones away to other herds while tending the rest. The breeder recognizes that efforts spent on animals corrupted by nature or poor upbringing will be wasted -- producing no improvement in either body or soul -- and that if left unaddressed, these animals will drag down and corrupt the healthy ones as well.

The case of animals is introduced only as an illustration; what applies to human beings matters far more. The lawgiver must investigate what kind of purification and procedure is appropriate for each situation. Consider the purification of a city: there are many methods, some easy and some difficult. The best and most difficult purifications can only be carried out by a legislator who also holds absolute power. A legislator who lacks despotic authority and is establishing a new government and legal system should count themselves fortunate if they can accomplish even the mildest form of purification. The best purification is painful, like the harsh remedies of medicine -- involving righteous punishment and, in extreme cases, death or exile.
elegant: A herdsman sorts animals before training them -- separating healthy from unhealthy, good breed from bad -- knowing that effort spent on the corrupted is wasted and that they will drag down the rest. The same applies to cities, only more urgently. The legislator must purify the population before building. Many methods exist, but the best and hardest require absolute power. A non-despotic legislator attempting even mild purification should count themselves fortunate to succeed. The strongest purification, like harsh medicine, involves righteous punishment -- and in the last resort, death or exile.
summary: Just as a herdsman purifies a flock before training it, a legislator must purify the citizenry before establishing laws. The most effective purification -- involving punishment, exile, or death -- requires absolute power; a non-despotic legislator can only attempt milder forms.
distilled: Before building a state, the legislator must purify the population as a herdsman culls a herd -- though the most effective purification requires absolute power most legislators lack.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The shepherd/herdsman analogy for the statesman appears in Republic I.343a-345e (Thrasymachus' challenge), Statesman 261d-268d (the extended shepherd analogy), and Gorgias 516a-b. The concept of civic 'purification' (katharsis) is troubling to modern readers but was a standard Greek political concept -- historical examples include Solon's reforms and colonial foundations. The distinction between the despotic legislator (who can effect full purification) and the non-despotic one (who must settle for milder measures) reflects the Laws' awareness of its own practical limitations compared to the Republic's ideal state, where philosopher-kings have absolute authority. The medical analogy for political reform is common in Plato -- cf. Gorgias 521e-522a, Statesman 293a-c.

narrative:
"For in this way we commonly dispose of great sinners who are incurable, and are the greatest injury of the whole state. But the milder form of purification is as follows: when men who have nothing, and are in want of food, show a disposition to follow their leaders in an attack on the property of the rich -- these, who are the natural plague of the state, are sent away by the legislator in a friendly spirit as far as he is able; and this dismissal of them is euphemistically termed a colony.

"And every legislator should contrive to do this at once. Our present case, however, is peculiar. For there is no need to devise any colony or purifying separation under the circumstances in which we are placed. But as, when many streams flow together from many sources, whether springs or mountain torrents, into a single lake, we ought to attend and take care that the confluent waters should be perfectly clear, and in order to effect this, should pump and draw off and divert impurities, so in every political arrangement there may be trouble and danger.

"But, seeing that we are now only discoursing and not acting, let our selection be supposed to be completed, and the desired purity attained. Touching evil men, who want to join and be citizens of our state, after we have tested them by every sort of persuasion and for a sufficient time, we will prevent them from coming; but the good we will to the utmost of our ability receive as friends with open arms."
refashioned:
Through severe purification we typically deal with great criminals who are beyond reform and who pose the gravest threat to the state as a whole. But there is also a milder form. When people who own nothing and lack food begin following agitators who would lead them to seize the property of the wealthy -- these people being the natural plague of any state -- the legislator sends them away as amicably as possible. This removal is politely called 'founding a colony.'

Every legislator should arrange for this immediately. Our situation, however, is unique. We have no need to organize a colony or purifying separation given our present circumstances. But consider the analogy of many streams flowing from various sources -- springs and mountain torrents -- into a single lake. We must ensure that the confluent waters are perfectly clean, pumping out, drawing off, and diverting any impurities. The same principle applies to every political arrangement, though trouble and danger may attend the process.

Since we are only theorizing and not yet acting, let us suppose the selection is complete and the desired purity has been achieved. As for wicked people who wish to join and become citizens of our state, we will test them through every kind of persuasion over a sufficient period of time and bar them from entry. But the good we will welcome as friends with open arms, to the fullest extent of our ability.
elegant: Severe purification removes incurable criminals. A milder form sends away the landless poor who threaten to seize the rich's property -- politely called 'founding a colony.' Our unique situation requires neither measure. But like streams flowing into a lake that must be kept clean by diverting impurities, every political arrangement must be purified of corrupting elements. Since we are theorizing, let us assume the purification complete. We will test would-be citizens thoroughly and bar the wicked, while warmly welcoming the good.
summary: Severe purification removes incurable criminals through punishment or death. Milder purification sends away the dangerous poor as 'colonists.' The Athenian's theoretical city requires neither method, but the principle remains: impurities must be diverted, the wicked excluded, and the good warmly welcomed.
distilled: The state must purify itself of corrupting elements -- removing incurable criminals, redirecting the dangerous poor as colonists, barring the wicked from citizenship, and welcoming the good.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The euphemistic description of colonization as a form of social 'purification' reflects actual Greek practice -- colonies (apoikiai) were often founded partly to relieve social pressure from landless poor (cf. the founding of Thurii, in which Plato's circle was involved). The threat of the poor attacking the property of the rich echoes Republic VIII.555c-557a on the oligarchic-democratic revolution. The water/streams metaphor for civic purity is original to the Laws. The theoretical idealization -- 'let us suppose the purification complete' -- acknowledges the gap between political theory and practice that the Laws repeatedly confronts. The openness to receiving good immigrants contrasts with many Greek cities' exclusionary policies and recalls the cosmopolitan tendencies in Plato's thought.

narrative:
"Another piece of good fortune must not be forgotten, which, as we were saying, the Heraclid colony had, and which is also ours -- that we have escaped division of land and the abolition of debts; for these are always a source of dangerous contention, and a city which is driven by necessity to legislate upon such matters can neither allow the old ways to continue, nor yet venture to alter them. We must have recourse to prayers, so to speak, and hope that a slight change may be cautiously effected in a length of time.

"And such a change can be accomplished by those who have abundance of land, and having also many debtors, are willing, in a kindly spirit, to share with those who are in want, sometimes remitting and sometimes giving, holding fast in a path of moderation, and deeming poverty to be the increase of a man's desires and not the diminution of his property. For this is the great beginning of salvation to a state, and upon this lasting basis may be erected afterwards whatever political order is suitable under the circumstances; but if the change be based upon an unsound principle, the future administration of the country will be full of difficulties.

"That is a danger which, as I am saying, is escaped by us, and yet we had better say how, if we had not escaped, we might have escaped; and we may venture now to assert that no other way of escape, whether narrow or broad, can be devised but freedom from avarice and a sense of justice -- upon this rock our city shall be built; for there ought to be no disputes among citizens about property. If there are quarrels of long standing among them, no legislator of any degree of sense will proceed a step in the arrangement of the state until they are settled. But that they to whom God has given, as he has to us, to be the founders of a new state as yet free from enmity -- that they should create themselves enmities by their mode of distributing lands and houses, would be superhuman folly and wickedness."
refashioned:
There is another stroke of good fortune we must not overlook -- one shared by the original Heraclid colony and by ourselves: we have been spared the crisis of land redistribution and debt cancellation.
