These issues are perpetual sources of dangerous conflict. A city forced to legislate on such matters can neither preserve the existing arrangements nor safely change them. In such situations, one can only resort to what amounts to prayer, hoping that gradual and cautious reform might be achieved over a long period.

Such change can be accomplished when those who possess abundant land and hold many debts are willing, in a generous spirit, to share with those in need -- sometimes forgiving debts, sometimes making outright gifts -- while maintaining a path of moderation. The key insight is that true poverty consists in the increase of a person's desires, not in the decrease of their property. This understanding is the foundation of a state's salvation, and upon it any suitable political order can later be erected. But if reform rests on an unsound principle, the future governance of the country will be plagued with difficulties.

We have escaped this danger, but let us still consider how we might have escaped it had circumstances been different. The answer is that no other path to safety exists -- whether wide or narrow -- except freedom from greed and a commitment to justice. On this rock our city shall be built, for there should be no disputes among citizens over property. Where longstanding quarrels about property exist, no sensible legislator will take a single step toward organizing the state until those quarrels are resolved. For founders whom God has blessed with a new state free from existing enmities to create enmities through their own method of distributing land and houses would be folly and wickedness beyond what is humanly imaginable.
elegant: We share the Heraclid colony's good fortune: we need not face the explosive issues of land redistribution and debt cancellation. Cities forced to legislate on such matters are trapped -- unable to maintain the status quo or safely change it. Gradual reform is possible only when the wealthy willingly share with those in need, understanding that poverty is really the growth of desire, not the shrinking of property. This insight is the foundation of political salvation. Our city must be built on freedom from greed and a sense of justice -- the only rock that holds. No wise legislator proceeds while property disputes remain unsettled, and founders blessed with a fresh start who create enmity through their own land distribution would be guilty of superhuman folly.
summary: The founders are fortunate to avoid the explosive issues of land redistribution and debt cancellation. True poverty is the increase of desire, not the decrease of property. The city must be built on freedom from greed and justice, since unresolved property disputes make political progress impossible.
distilled: The city must be built on freedom from avarice and a sense of justice, for true poverty lies in the growth of desire, not the shrinking of possessions.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The Heraclid colony refers to the Dorian settlement of the Peloponnese, discussed in Laws III (682d-693e). Land redistribution (gēs anadasmos) and debt cancellation (chreōn apokopē) were the two most revolutionary demands in Greek politics -- cf. Solon's seisachtheia in Athens (594 BC). The definition of poverty as 'increase of desires rather than diminution of property' is a profound reorientation that anticipates Epicurean and Stoic economics. The phrase 'upon this rock our city shall be built' uses architectural metaphor for justice as foundation, paralleling Republic IV.433a-434c (justice as the foundation of the ideal state). The warning against creating enmities through distribution connects to Republic V.462a-e on unity as the greatest good for a state.

narrative:
"How then can we rightly order the distribution of the land? In the first place, the number of the citizens has to be determined, and also the number and size of the divisions into which they will have to be formed; and the land and the houses will then have to be apportioned by us as fairly as we can. The number of citizens can only be estimated satisfactorily in relation to the territory and the neighbouring states. The territory must be sufficient to maintain a certain number of inhabitants in a moderate way of life -- more than this is not required; and the number of citizens should be sufficient to defend themselves against the injustice of their neighbours, and also to give them the power of rendering efficient aid to their neighbours when they are wronged.

"After having taken a survey of theirs and their neighbours' territory, we will determine the limits of them in fact as well as in theory. And now, let us proceed to legislate with a view to perfecting the form and outline of our state. The number of our citizens shall be 5040 -- this will be a convenient number; and these shall be owners of the land and protectors of the allotment. The houses and the land will be divided in the same way, so that every man may correspond to a lot.

"Let the whole number be first divided into two parts, and then into three; and the number is further capable of being divided into four or five parts, or any number of parts up to ten. Every legislator ought to know so much arithmetic as to be able to tell what number is most likely to be useful to all cities; and we are going to take that number which contains the greatest and most regular and unbroken series of divisions."
refashioned:
How, then, should we properly organize the distribution of land? First, the number of citizens must be determined, along with the number and size of the divisions they will be organized into. Then the land and houses must be apportioned as equitably as possible.

The appropriate number of citizens can only be calculated in relation to the available territory and the neighboring states. The territory must sustain a certain number of inhabitants living moderately -- nothing more is needed. The number of citizens must be large enough for self-defense against unjust neighbors and also sufficient to provide effective aid to neighbors who are wronged.

After surveying both our territory and our neighbors', we will set these boundaries in practice as well as in theory. Now let us proceed to legislate toward perfecting our state's design. The number of citizens shall be 5,040 -- a highly convenient number. These citizens shall be the owners of the land and guardians of their respective allotments. Houses and land will be divided correspondingly, so that each citizen has a designated lot.

The total can first be divided into two parts, then three, and further into four, five, or any number of parts up to ten. Every legislator should understand enough arithmetic to identify the number most useful for organizing a city. We have chosen the number that allows the greatest, most regular, and most unbroken series of divisions.
elegant: Land distribution requires first fixing the number of citizens relative to the territory and neighboring states. The territory must sustain a moderate way of life; the population must be large enough for defense and mutual aid. The number shall be 5,040 -- chosen because it admits the greatest and most regular series of divisions, being divisible into two, three, four, five, and every number up to ten. Each citizen receives one lot; houses and land are divided correspondingly. Every legislator needs sufficient arithmetic to select the most useful number for organizing a city.
summary: The number of citizens is set at 5,040, chosen for its exceptional mathematical divisibility. The territory must sustain moderate living and provide sufficient population for defense. Each citizen receives a corresponding lot of land and housing.
distilled: The state shall have 5,040 citizen-landholders -- a number chosen for its unmatched mathematical divisibility, enabling flexible organization of all civic, military, and economic arrangements.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The number 5,040 equals 7! (7 factorial = 7x6x5x4x3x2x1) and has 59 divisors, making it exceptionally useful for administrative divisions. This reflects Plato's Pythagorean convictions about the governance-mathematics connection. The moderate self-sufficiency of the territory echoes Aristotle's later principle in Politics VII.4-5 (1325b-1327a). The dual military purpose -- defense and aiding neighbors -- reflects the principle of reciprocal justice among states. The insistence on arithmetic knowledge for legislators connects to Republic VII.522c-526c on mathematics as essential preparation for rulers. The fixed number of lots with corresponding citizen-owners establishes the property system that the remainder of Book V will elaborate.

narrative:
"The whole of number has every possible division, and the number 5040 can be divided by exactly fifty-nine divisors, and ten of these proceed without interval from one to ten: this will furnish numbers for war and peace, and for all contracts and dealings, including taxes and divisions of the land. These properties of number should be ascertained at leisure by those who are bound by law to know them; for they are true, and should be proclaimed at the foundation of the city, with a view to use.

"Whether the legislator is establishing a new state or restoring an old and decayed one, in respect of Gods and temples -- the temples which are to be built in each city, and the Gods or demi-gods after whom they are to be called -- if he be a man of sense, he will make no change in anything which the oracle of Delphi, or Dodona, or the God Ammon, or any ancient tradition has sanctioned in whatever manner, whether by apparitions or reputed inspiration of Heaven, in obedience to which mankind have established sacrifices in connection with mystic rites, either originating on the spot, or derived from Tyrrhenia or Cyprus or some other place, and on the strength of which traditions they have consecrated oracles and images, and altars and temples, and portioned out a sacred domain for each of them.

"The least part of all these ought not to be disturbed by the legislator; but he should assign to the several districts some God, or demi-god, or hero, and, in the distribution of the soil, should give to these first their chosen domain and all things fitting, that the inhabitants of the several districts may meet at fixed times, and that they may readily supply their various wants, and entertain one another with sacrifices, and become friends and acquaintances; for there is no greater good in a state than that the citizens should be known to one another. When not light but darkness and ignorance of each other's characters prevails among them, no one will receive the honour of which he is deserving, or the power or the justice to which he is fairly entitled: wherefore, in every state, above all things, every man should take heed that he have no deceit in him, but that he be always true and simple; and that no deceitful person take any advantage of him."
refashioned:
The number 5,040 can be divided by exactly fifty-nine divisors, and ten of these form an unbroken sequence from one to ten. This provides ready-made numbers for military organization, peacetime administration, contracts, commercial dealings, taxation, and land division. These mathematical properties should be studied carefully by those whom the law requires to understand them, since they are factual truths that should be publicly declared at the city's founding for practical application.

Whether establishing a new state or restoring a decayed one, when it comes to gods and temples -- which temples to build in each district and which gods or demigods they should be named after -- a sensible legislator will alter nothing that has been sanctioned by the oracle at Delphi, Dodona, the god Ammon, or any ancient tradition. Whether these traditions arose through divine visions, reported heavenly inspiration, or mystic rites originating locally or derived from Etruria, Cyprus, or elsewhere -- and whether they gave rise to consecrated oracles, sacred images, altars, temples, or sacred domains -- the legislator must not disturb even the smallest part of them.

Instead, each district should be assigned a patron god, demigod, or hero, and in distributing the land, sacred domains should be allotted first with all proper provisions. This ensures that the inhabitants of each district will gather at regular intervals, supply one another's needs, share in sacrificial festivals, and become friends and acquaintances. There is no greater good for a state than that its citizens know one another. When darkness and mutual ignorance of character prevail, no one receives the honor, power, or justice they deserve. Therefore, above all else, every person should ensure they harbor no deceit but remain always truthful and straightforward, and should guard against being taken advantage of by the deceitful.
elegant: The number 5,040 has fifty-nine divisors, including every number from one to ten consecutively -- providing ready-made divisions for war, peace, taxes, contracts, and land. These properties should be publicly declared at the city's founding. Regarding temples and gods, a wise legislator changes nothing sanctioned by Delphi, Dodona, Ammon, or any ancient tradition. Each district should receive a patron deity and sacred domain first, so inhabitants gather regularly for festivals, supply mutual needs, and form friendships. No greater good exists for a state than citizens knowing one another's character. Where mutual ignorance reigns, no one receives deserved honor or justice. Therefore, cultivate truthfulness and guard against deceit.
summary: The number 5,040 provides exceptional divisibility for all civic purposes. Religious traditions should be preserved unchanged. Each district needs a patron deity and regular festivals to foster the mutual acquaintance among citizens that is the greatest good of any state.
distilled: Sacred traditions must be preserved, each district given a patron deity and regular festivals, because the greatest good of any state is that citizens truly know one another's character.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The mathematical discussion reflects the deep Pythagorean influence on late Plato -- cf. Timaeus 35b-36d on mathematical structure in the cosmos. The deference to Delphi, Dodona, and Ammon for religious matters echoes Republic IV.427b-c, where Socrates delegates all religious legislation to Apollo at Delphi. Tyrrhenia (Etruria) and Cyprus were known sources of mystery religions. The principle that citizens must know one another is fundamental to Plato's political theory and explains the small scale of the ideal city -- cf. Republic V.462a-e on unity through mutual knowledge. Aristotle develops this further in Politics VII.4 (1326b), arguing the state must be 'surveyable' (eusynoptos). The emphasis on transparency and against deceit returns to the truth-theme of 5.8.

narrative:
"The next move in our pastime of legislation, like the withdrawal of the stone from the holy line in the game of draughts, being an unusual one, will probably excite wonder when mentioned for the first time. And yet, if a man will only reflect and weigh the matter with care, he will see that our city is ordered in a manner which, if not the best, is the second best.

"Perhaps also some one may not approve this form, because he thinks that such a constitution is ill adapted to a legislator who has not despotic power. The truth is, that there are three forms of government, the best, the second and the third best, which we may just mention, and then leave the selection to the ruler of the settlement. Following this method in the present instance, let us speak of the states which are respectively first, second, and third in excellence, and then we will leave the choice to Cleinias now, or to any one else who may hereafter have to make a similar choice among constitutions, and may desire to give to his state some feature which is congenial to him and which he approves in his own country."
refashioned:
The next step in our legislative exercise -- like making an unusual move in a game of draughts by withdrawing a piece from the sacred line -- will likely provoke surprise when first mentioned. Yet anyone who reflects on it carefully will recognize that our city is organized in a manner that is, if not the absolute best, then the second best.

Some may object to this form of constitution, believing it unsuited to a legislator who lacks absolute power. But the truth is that there are three grades of government -- the best, the second best, and the third best. Let us briefly describe each and then leave the selection to the ruler of the settlement. In this spirit, let us outline the states that rank first, second, and third in excellence, and leave the final choice to Cleinias -- or to anyone else who may someday face a similar constitutional decision and wish to incorporate features that suit their own temperament and the traditions of their homeland.
elegant: The next legislative move may seem startling, but careful reflection shows our city's organization to be the second best, if not the best. Three grades of government exist -- best, second best, and third best. Let us describe each and leave the selection to the founder. Cleinias, or any future constitution-maker, can then choose what suits their circumstances and the traditions of their homeland.
summary: The Athenian prepares to introduce an unconventional feature of the constitution, noting that there are three grades of government. He will describe each and leave the choice to Cleinias or any future founder.
distilled: Three grades of government exist -- best, second best, and third best -- and the Athenian will describe each before leaving the constitutional choice to the settlement's founder.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 'game of draughts' (pesseia) metaphor -- specifically moving a piece from the 'sacred line' (hiera gramme) -- was a proverbial expression for a bold or irreversible move. The ranking of constitutions as first, second, and third best is unique to the Laws and represents Plato's late political realism. The 'first best' state (described in 5.23) is the communistic Republic; the 'second best' is the Laws' own project. The deference to Cleinias as the actual settler marks the Laws' distinctive respect for practical political agency, unlike the Republic's more purely theoretical approach. The acknowledgment that a legislator without despotic power faces constraints echoes 5.17 and reflects the dialogue's consistent theme of political realism.

narrative:
"The first and highest form of the state and of the government and of the law is that in which there prevails most widely the ancient saying, that 'Friends have all things in common.' Whether there is anywhere now, or will ever be, this communion of women and children and of property, in which the private and individual is altogether banished from life, and things which are by nature private, such as eyes and ears and hands, have become common, and in some way see and hear and act in common, and all men express praise and blame and feel joy and sorrow on the same occasions, and whatever laws there are unite the city to the utmost -- whether all this is possible or not, I say that no man, acting upon any other principle, will ever constitute a state which will be truer or better or more exalted in virtue.

"Whether such a state is governed by Gods or sons of Gods, one, or more than one, happy are the men who, living after this manner, dwell there; and therefore to this we are to look for the pattern of the state, and to cling to this, and to seek with all our might for one which is like this. The state which we have now in hand, when created, will be nearest to immortality and the only one which takes the second place; and after that, by the grace of God, we will complete the third one. And we will begin by speaking of the nature and origin of the second."
refashioned:
The first and highest form of state, government, and law is the one that most fully realizes the ancient principle: 'Friends have all things in common.' Whether such a community of women, children, and property exists anywhere now or ever will -- one in which the private and individual is entirely abolished from life, where even things naturally private, like eyes and ears and hands, somehow see and hear and act in common, where all people express the same praise and blame and feel the same joy and sorrow on the same occasions, and where laws unite the city to the greatest possible degree -- whether all this is achievable or not, no one operating on any other principle will ever build a state that is truer, better, or more exalted in virtue.

Whether such a state is governed by gods or the children of gods, by one ruler or many, those who live in it are blessed. This is the pattern we must look to, the ideal we must cling to, the model we must strive with all our strength to approximate. The state we are now designing will come nearest to this immortal ideal and will hold second place -- and afterward, with God's help, we will describe the third. Let us begin by discussing the nature and origin of this second-best state.
elegant: The highest form of state fully realizes the principle that friends share all things in common -- property, women, children, even perception and emotion unified so the city acts as one. Whether achievable or not, no other principle can produce a truer, better, or more virtuous state. This ideal is the pattern we must approximate. Our current project -- the second-best state -- will come nearest to this immortal model. We will describe the third form afterward.
summary: The ideal state is one of total communism where friends share everything -- property, family, even perception and feeling. Whether achievable or not, it remains the standard against which all constitutions must be measured. The state being designed in the Laws is the second-best approximation of this ideal.
distilled: The highest political ideal is total community of property, family, and even feeling -- and though it may be unachievable, the state we are designing must approximate it as closely as possible.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is Plato's most explicit cross-reference to the Republic's communism of property, women, and children (Republic V.449a-471c). The saying 'friends have all things in common' (koina ta philōn) was attributed to Pythagoras and appears in Republic IV.424a and Phaedrus 279c. The vision of unified perception -- eyes, ears, hands acting in common -- pushes the Republic's ideal toward an almost mystical organicism that goes beyond anything in the earlier dialogue. The ranking of the Republic's state as 'first best' and the Laws' state as 'second best' is the Laws' definitive statement of its relationship to the Republic. The acknowledgment that the ideal may not be 'possible' echoes Republic V.472c-473b, where Socrates admits the ideal state may never exist. The phrase 'nearest to immortality' for the second-best state suggests that approximating the divine pattern is the most mortals can achieve.

narrative:
"Let the citizens at once distribute their land and houses, and not till the land in common, since a community of goods goes beyond their proposed origin, and nurture, and education. But in making the distribution, let the several possessors feel that their particular lots also belong to the whole city; and seeing that the earth is their parent, let them tend her more carefully than children do their mother. For she is a goddess and their queen, and they are her mortal subjects.

"Such also are the feelings which they ought to entertain to the Gods and demi-gods of the country. And in order that the distribution may always remain, they ought to consider further that the present number of families should be always retained, and neither increased nor diminished. This may be secured for the whole city in the following manner: Let the possessor of a lot leave the one of his children who is his best beloved, and one only, to be the heir of his dwelling, and his successor in the duty of ministering to the Gods, the state and the family, as well the living members of it as those who are departed when he comes into the inheritance; but of his other children, if he have more than one, he shall give the females in marriage according to the law to be hereafter enacted, and the males he shall distribute as sons to those citizens who have no children and are disposed to receive them; or if there should be none such, and particular individuals have too many children, male or female, or too few, as in the case of barrenness -- in all these cases let the highest and most honourable magistracy created by us judge and determine what is to be done with the redundant or deficient, and devise a means that the number of 5040 houses shall always remain the same.

"There are many ways of regulating numbers; for they in whom generation is affluent may be made to refrain, and, on the other hand, special care may be taken to increase the number of births by rewards and stigmas, or we may meet the evil by the elder men giving advice and administering rebuke to the younger -- in this way the object may be attained."
refashioned:
Citizens should immediately distribute the land and houses among themselves rather than farming in common, since complete communal ownership exceeds what their particular origins, upbringing, and education have prepared them for. However, in making this distribution, each possessor should feel that their individual lot also belongs to the city as a whole. The earth is their parent, and they should tend her more carefully than children care for their mother -- for she is a goddess and their sovereign, and they are her mortal subjects. The same reverence should extend to the gods and demigods of the land.

To ensure the distribution remains permanent, the number of households must be kept constant -- neither increased nor decreased. This can be achieved as follows: each landholder should designate one child -- their most beloved -- as sole heir to the dwelling and successor in the duties of serving the gods, the state, and the family, including both the living and the departed. Other daughters should be given in marriage according to laws yet to be enacted. Other sons should be adopted out to childless citizens willing to receive them.

Where adoption is not possible and some families have too many children or too few -- whether from fertility or barrenness -- the highest and most honorable magistracy we establish shall judge each case, devising means to ensure that the number of 5,040 households always remains the same. There are many methods for regulating population: those with abundant fertility can be encouraged to restrain themselves, while births can be stimulated through incentives and social pressure, or elders can counsel and admonish the young. Through such measures, the objective can be achieved.
elegant: Citizens should divide land and houses individually rather than hold them in common -- full communism exceeds their preparation. Yet each possessor must feel their lot belongs to the whole city, tending the earth as their divine mother, goddess, and sovereign. The same reverence extends to the local gods and demigods. To preserve the distribution permanently, the number of households must remain fixed at exactly 5,040. One beloved child inherits each lot as sole heir, succeeding to the duties of serving gods, state, and family -- both living and departed. Surplus daughters are given in marriage; surplus sons are adopted out to childless families. Where imbalances in family size persist, the highest magistracy intervenes to judge each case, regulating population through restraint of the fertile, incentives and social pressure to increase births, or counsel from elders to the young.
summary: Land should be distributed individually rather than held in common, but possessors must view their lots as belonging to the whole city. The number of households must remain fixed at 5,040 through single-heir inheritance, adoption of surplus children, and population regulation by magistrates.
distilled: Each of the 5,040 lots passes to a single heir, with surplus children adopted out and population regulated by magistrates -- ensuring the fixed number of households endures permanently.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The retreat from communal property to individual lots with communal consciousness represents the Laws' 'second-best' compromise with the Republic's communism (Republic V.449a-466d). The reverence for earth as divine mother reflects Hesiod's Theogony (Gaia) and the autochthony myth used in Republic III.414d-415d (the 'noble lie' that citizens are born from the earth). The single-heir inheritance system, with adoption as a population-balancing mechanism, was influenced by actual Spartan practices (the Spartan kleros system). Population regulation through magistrates anticipates Aristotle's discussion in Politics VII.16 (1335a-1335b). The concern for maintaining exactly 5,040 households reflects the mathematical rigor established in 5.20-5.21.

narrative:
"And if after all there be very great difficulty about the equal preservation of the 5040 houses, and there be an excess of citizens, owing to the too great love of those who live together, and we are at our wits' end, there is still the old device often mentioned by us of sending out a colony, which will part friends with us, and be composed of suitable persons.

"If, on the other hand, there come a wave bearing a deluge of disease, or a plague of war, and the inhabitants become much fewer than the appointed number by reason of bereavement, we ought not to introduce citizens of spurious birth and education, if this can be avoided; but even God is said not to be able to fight against necessity."
refashioned:
If, despite all these measures, maintaining exactly 5,040 households proves extremely difficult and an excess of citizens accumulates -- due to the excessive fertility that comes from the affection of those who live together -- and we find ourselves at a loss, there remains the old expedient we have mentioned before: founding a colony. This colony will depart on friendly terms and consist of suitable individuals.

Conversely, if a devastating wave of disease or the ravages of war reduce the inhabitants far below the appointed number through mass bereavement, we should avoid introducing citizens of inferior birth and education if at all possible. But even God, it is said, cannot fight against necessity.
elegant: If the 5,040 households cannot be maintained and population grows excessive, the remedy is colonization -- sending out settlers on friendly terms. If plague or war reduces the population drastically, citizens of inferior birth and education should not be admitted if avoidable. But even God, it is said, cannot fight against necessity.
summary: If population exceeds 5,040 households, surplus citizens should be sent out as colonists on friendly terms. If plague or war drastically reduces the population, admitting citizens of inferior quality should be a last resort -- but even God cannot fight necessity.
distilled: Colonization handles population surplus and necessity may force accepting lesser citizens after catastrophe, for even God cannot fight against necessity.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The colonization solution echoes the 'milder purification' of 5.18 and reflects actual Greek practice -- overpopulation was a primary driver of colonization from the 8th century BC onward. The proverb 'even God cannot fight against necessity' (ananke) is attributed to various sources including Simonides and Pittacus (one of the Seven Sages). Necessity (ananke) is a fundamental concept in Greek thought -- cf. Timaeus 47e-48a, where necessity is the co-cause alongside intelligence in the creation of the cosmos. Republic III.415a-c similarly deals with the problem of maintaining class quality while acknowledging that nature sometimes produces different types. The reluctance to admit 'spurious' citizens reflects the Greek concept of eugeneia (good birth) -- cf. Republic V.459a-461e on eugenic breeding among guardians.

narrative:
"Wherefore let us suppose this 'high argument' of ours to address us in the following terms: Best of men, cease not to honour according to nature similarity and equality and sameness and agreement, as regards number and every good and noble quality. And, above all, observe the aforesaid number 5040 throughout life; in the second place, do not disparage the small and modest proportions of the inheritances which you received in the distribution, by buying and selling them to one another. For then neither will the God who gave you the lot be your friend, nor will the legislator; and indeed the law declares to the disobedient that these are the terms upon which he may or may not take the lot.

"In the first place, the earth as he is informed is sacred to the Gods; and in the next place, priests and priestesses will offer up prayers over a first, and second, and even a third sacrifice, that he who buys or sells the houses or lands which he has received, may suffer the punishment which he deserves; and these their prayers they shall write down in the temples, on tablets of cypress-wood, for the instruction of posterity.

"Moreover they will set a watch over all these things, that they may be observed; the magistracy which has the sharpest eyes shall keep watch that any infringement of these commands may be discovered and punished as offences both against the law and the God. How great is the benefit of such an ordinance to all those cities, which obey and are administered accordingly, no bad man can ever know, as the old proverb says; but only a man of experience and good habits.

"For in such an order of things there will not be much opportunity for making money; no man either ought, or indeed will be allowed, to exercise any ignoble occupation, of which the vulgarity is a matter of reproach to a freeman, and should never want to acquire riches by any such means."
refashioned:
Let us imagine our 'high argument' addressing us directly: 'Best of men, never cease honoring -- in accordance with nature -- similarity, equality, sameness, and harmony, both in number and in every good and noble quality. Above all, maintain the number 5,040 throughout your lives. And do not devalue the modest proportions of the inheritances you received in the original distribution by buying and selling them among yourselves. If you do, neither the God who assigned you the lot nor the legislator will be your friend. The law declares to the disobedient that these are the conditions under which the lot is granted or withheld.'

First, the citizen must understand that the earth is sacred to the gods. Second, priests and priestesses will perform prayers over a first, second, and even third sacrifice, invoking due punishment on anyone who buys or sells the houses or lands they received. These prayers shall be inscribed in the temples on tablets of cypress-wood as a warning for future generations.

Furthermore, a watch will be set over all these matters to ensure compliance. The magistracy with the sharpest oversight shall monitor for any violation, and transgressions shall be punished as offenses against both the law and the divine order. How great the benefit of such an ordinance is to cities that obey it, no wicked person can ever understand, as the old proverb says -- only a person of experience and good character.

Under such an arrangement, there will be little opportunity for accumulating wealth. No citizen should -- or will be permitted to -- pursue any degrading occupation whose vulgarity would be a disgrace to a free person, nor should anyone seek to grow rich by such means.
elegant: The argument itself speaks to the citizens: honor natural equality, sameness, and harmony, and maintain the number 5,040 throughout life. Never buy or sell the lots you received in the distribution, for the earth is sacred to the gods. Priests and priestesses will invoke curses on any who traffic in houses or land, inscribing their prayers on cypress-wood tablets in the temples as a permanent warning for posterity. The sharpest-eyed magistracy will enforce this, punishing all violations as offenses against both the law and divine order. How great the benefit of such an ordinance, no wicked person can ever comprehend -- only the experienced and virtuous understand. Under this system, little opportunity exists for accumulating money, and no citizen should pursue any degrading occupation whose vulgarity would disgrace a free person.
summary: Lots of land must never be bought or sold -- the earth is sacred, and priests will invoke curses on violators inscribed on temple tablets. Magistrates will enforce this prohibition. Under such a system, there is little opportunity for wealth accumulation, and no free citizen should pursue degrading occupations.
distilled: Land allotments are sacred and inalienable -- priests will curse violators, magistrates will punish them, and the system deliberately limits opportunities for wealth accumulation.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The personification of the argument addressing the citizens is a rhetorical device used throughout the Laws (cf. the Laws speaking in the Crito 50a-54d). The inalienability of land lots was a feature of some historical Greek constitutions, notably Sparta's legendary Lycurgan system (Plutarch, Lycurgus 8). The inscribing of curses on cypress-wood tablets in temples combines religious and legal enforcement -- a distinctively Laws approach that integrates theology into governance. The prohibition against 'ignoble occupations' (banausic trades) for free citizens reflects widespread Greek elite attitudes (cf. Aristotle, Politics III.5, 1278a) and connects to the Republic's guardian class restrictions (III.415a-417b). The proverb about bad men not understanding good governance echoes the philosopher-knowledge theme of Republic VII.

narrative:
"Further, the law enjoins that no private man shall be allowed to possess gold and silver, but only coin for daily use, which is almost necessary in dealing with artisans, and for payment of hirelings, whether slaves or immigrants, by all those persons who require the use of them. Wherefore our citizens, as we say, should have a coin passing current among themselves, but not accepted among the rest of mankind; with a view, however, to expeditions and journeys to other lands -- for embassies, or for any other occasion which may arise of sending out a herald, the state must also possess a common Hellenic currency.

"If a private person is ever obliged to go abroad, let him have the consent of the magistrates and go; and if when he returns he has any foreign money remaining, let him give the surplus back to the treasury, and receive a corresponding sum in the local currency. And if he is discovered to appropriate it, let it be confiscated, and let him who knows and does not inform be subject to curse and dishonour equally with him who brought the money, and also to a fine not less in amount than the foreign money which has been brought back.

"In marrying and giving in marriage, no one shall give or receive any dowry at all; and no one shall deposit money with another whom he does not trust as a friend, nor shall he lend money upon interest; and the borrower should be under no obligation to repay either capital or interest. That these principles are best, any one may see who compares them with the first principle and intention of a state."
refashioned:
The law further stipulates that no private citizen may possess gold or silver -- only local coinage for everyday transactions, which is virtually essential for dealings with craftsmen and for paying wages to hired workers, whether slaves or immigrants. Citizens shall therefore use a currency that circulates among themselves but has no value abroad.

However, for military expeditions, diplomatic missions, embassies, and any other occasion requiring travel to foreign lands, the state itself must maintain a reserve of standard Greek currency. If a private citizen must travel abroad, they shall first obtain permission from the magistrates. Upon returning, any remaining foreign currency must be surrendered to the treasury in exchange for an equivalent sum in local coin. Anyone caught keeping foreign money shall have it confiscated. Furthermore, anyone who knows of such an offense and fails to report it shall face the same curse and dishonor as the offender, plus a fine at least equal to the amount of foreign currency involved.

In matters of marriage, no one shall give or receive any dowry whatsoever. No one shall deposit money with another person they do not trust as a genuine friend. No one shall lend money at interest. And borrowers shall be under no obligation to repay either principal or interest. Anyone can see that these principles are sound by comparing them with the fundamental purpose and intention of a state.
elegant: No private citizen may possess gold or silver -- only local coin for daily commerce with artisans and hired workers. This currency has no value abroad. The state alone maintains foreign currency for diplomatic and military purposes. Citizens traveling abroad need magistrate approval and must surrender remaining foreign money upon return or face confiscation; those who fail to report violations share the same penalties. No dowries in marriage. No lending at interest. No obligation to repay loans of capital or interest. These principles align with the fundamental purpose of the state.
summary: Private citizens may only possess local currency of no foreign value. The state alone holds Greek currency for official purposes. Foreign money must be surrendered upon return from travel. Marriage dowries are prohibited, as is lending at interest -- borrowers bear no obligation to repay.
distilled: Private gold and silver are banned, only local currency is permitted, dowries are prohibited, and lending at interest is abolished -- all to prevent wealth accumulation from undermining the state's purpose.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The dual currency system -- local coin without foreign value, plus state-held Greek currency -- is one of the Laws' most distinctive economic proposals. It parallels Sparta's iron currency (Plutarch, Lycurgus 9), which similarly aimed to discourage commerce and wealth accumulation. The prohibition on gold and silver for private citizens echoes Republic III.416d-417b (guardians may not touch gold/silver). The ban on interest-bearing loans reflects widespread ancient criticism of usury -- cf. Aristotle, Politics I.10 (1258b), where money-lending is called the most unnatural form of acquisition. The abolition of dowries aims to prevent marriage from becoming a financial transaction. The remarkable provision that borrowers need not repay capital or interest makes lending essentially a gift -- radically discouraging the credit economy. The information requirement (reporting violations) creates a system of civic mutual surveillance that recalls Sparta's institutions.

narrative:
"The intention, as we affirm, of a reasonable statesman, is not what the many declare to be the object of a good legislator, namely, that the state for the true interests of which he is advising should be as great and as rich as possible, and should possess gold and silver, and have the greatest empire by sea and land; this they imagine to be the real object of legislation, at the same time adding, inconsistently, that the true legislator desires to have the city the best and happiest possible.

"But they do not see that some of these things are possible, and some of them are impossible; and he who orders the state will desire what is possible, and will not indulge in vain wishes or attempts to accomplish that which is impossible. The citizen must indeed be happy and good, and the legislator will seek to make him so; but very rich and very good at the same time he cannot be, not, at least, in the sense in which the many speak of riches. For they mean by 'the rich' the few who have the most valuable possessions, although the owner of them may quite well be a rogue."
refashioned:
The true aim of a reasonable statesman is not what the masses declare to be the purpose of good legislation -- namely, that the state should be made as large and wealthy as possible, overflowing with gold and silver, commanding the greatest empire on land and sea. This is what people imagine legislation exists to achieve, and they add, quite inconsistently, that the true lawgiver should also make the city as virtuous and happy as possible.

What they fail to see is that some of these goals are achievable while others are not, and a sensible organizer of the state will pursue only what is possible, refusing to waste effort on impossibilities. Citizens must indeed be happy and good -- this the legislator will work to ensure. But a person cannot be both extremely wealthy and extremely good at the same time, at least not in the way most people understand wealth. When the masses speak of 'the rich,' they mean the few who possess the most valuable property -- though the owner of such property may very well be a scoundrel.
elegant: A reasonable statesman does not aim for what the masses demand -- maximum size, wealth, gold, silver, and empire. People inconsistently add that the state should also be virtuous and happy. But some goals are compatible and others are not. A wise legislator pursues only what is possible. Citizens must be happy and good, but they cannot be both extremely wealthy and extremely good. The masses define 'rich' as those with the most valuable possessions -- though such owners may well be scoundrels.
summary: The true legislator does not aim for maximum wealth and empire but for citizens who are happy and good. Extreme wealth and true goodness are incompatible, since the masses define 'rich' as possessing the most -- regardless of the owner's moral character.
distilled: A wise legislator pursues goodness and happiness for citizens rather than maximum wealth and empire, because extreme riches and genuine virtue are incompatible.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage directly challenges the imperialist ideology of Athenian democracy -- the pursuit of wealth and naval empire that Thucydides chronicles in the History of the Peloponnesian War. The argument that a person cannot be both very rich and very good echoes Republic I.330d-331b (Cephalus' moderate view of wealth) and anticipates the detailed proof in 5.29. The critique of popular opinion about legislation's purpose parallels the philosopher-king argument in Republic V-VII -- the masses have fundamentally wrong ideas about what constitutes political success. The term 'rogue' for the wealthy owner connects to Republic VIII.553a-555b on the oligarchic character, driven by acquisitiveness regardless of justice.

narrative:
"And if this is true, I can never assent to the doctrine that the rich man will be happy -- he must be good as well as rich. And good in a high degree, and rich in a high degree at the same time, he cannot be. Some one will ask, why not? And we shall answer: Because acquisitions which come from sources which are just and unjust indifferently, are more than double those which come from just sources only; and the sums which are expended neither honourably nor disgracefully, are only half as great as those which are expended honourably and on honourable purposes.

"Thus, if the one acquires double and spends half, the other who is in the opposite case and is a good man cannot possibly be wealthier than he. The first -- I am speaking of the saver and not of the spender -- is not always bad; he may indeed in some cases be utterly bad, but, as I was saying, a good man he never is. For he who receives money unjustly as well as justly, and spends neither honourably nor dishonourably, will be a rich man if he be also thrifty. On the other hand, the utterly bad is in general profligate, and therefore very poor; while he who spends on noble objects, and acquires wealth by just means only, can hardly be remarkable for riches, any more than he can be very poor.

"Our statement, then, is true, that the very rich are not good, and, if they are not good, they are not happy."
refashioned:
If this is so, I cannot accept the view that the rich person will necessarily be happy -- they must also be good. But a person cannot be both highly virtuous and highly wealthy at the same time. Someone might ask: why not? Here is the answer.

A person who acquires wealth from both just and unjust sources indiscriminately accumulates more than double what someone who earns only through just means can gather. Meanwhile, a person who spends without concern for honor or dishonor saves roughly half as much as one who directs spending only toward honorable purposes. So if the indiscriminate earner acquires double and saves half, the honest person in the opposite position can never match their wealth.

The indiscriminate earner and saver is not always thoroughly wicked -- they may be in some cases -- but they are never truly good. Someone who takes money from both just and unjust sources and spends it in morally neutral ways will be rich if they are also frugal. The thoroughly bad person, on the other hand, tends to be extravagant and therefore quite poor. And the genuinely good person, who earns only through just means and spends on noble causes, can hardly accumulate remarkable wealth -- though they will not be destitute either.

Our claim therefore stands: the very rich are not good, and if they are not good, they are not happy.
elegant: The rich person is not necessarily happy -- they must be good as well. But extreme wealth and high virtue are incompatible. Why? Those who acquire from both just and unjust sources gain more than double what the just earner gains. Those who spend without moral concern save far more than those who spend honorably. The morally indifferent accumulator grows wealthy; the thoroughly bad person is usually extravagant and poor; the genuinely good person, earning justly and spending nobly, can never match the wealth of the unscrupulous. Therefore: the very rich are not good, and if not good, not happy.
summary: The Athenian proves mathematically that extreme wealth and virtue are incompatible. The unscrupulous person earns from all sources and spends cheaply, outpacing the virtuous person who earns only justly and spends nobly. Therefore the very rich cannot be good, and without goodness, they cannot be happy.
distilled: The very rich cannot be good because moral constraints on earning and spending inevitably limit accumulation -- and without goodness, there is no happiness.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is one of Plato's most remarkable economic arguments -- a quasi-mathematical proof that structural features of just vs. unjust acquisition make extreme wealth and virtue incompatible. The logic anticipates modern arguments about competitive disadvantage for ethical actors in unregulated markets. The conclusion that 'the very rich are not good' echoes Jesus' later teaching about the difficulty of the rich entering the kingdom of heaven (Mark 10:25), though from different premises. In Plato, cf. Republic I.330d (Cephalus: moderate wealth and a clear conscience), Republic VIII.550d-555a (oligarchic wealth-obsession as moral corruption), and Gorgias 470d-471d (Polus' claim that the tyrant is happy, which Socrates refutes). The three-way typology -- morally neutral accumulator, profligate bad person, and moderate good person -- is original to the Laws.

narrative:
"But the intention of our laws was that the citizens should be as happy as may be, and as friendly as possible to one another. And men who are always at law with one another, and amongst whom there are many wrongs done, can never be friends to one another, but only those among whom crimes and lawsuits are few and slight.

"Therefore we say that gold and silver ought not to be allowed in the city, nor much of the vulgar sort of trade which is carried on by lending money, or rearing the meaner kinds of live stock; but only the produce of agriculture, and only so much of this as will not compel us in pursuing it to neglect that for the sake of which riches exist -- I mean, soul and body, which without gymnastics, and without education, will never be worth anything; and therefore, as we have said not once but many times, the care of riches should have the last place in our thoughts.

"For there are in all three things about which every man has an interest; and the interest about money, when rightly regarded, is the third and lowest of them: midway comes the interest of the body; and, first of all, that of the soul; and the state which we are describing will have been rightly constituted if it ordains honours according to this scale. But if, in any of the laws which have been ordained, health has been preferred to temperance, or wealth to health and temperate habits, that law must clearly be wrong. Wherefore, also, the legislator ought often to impress upon himself the question -- 'What do I want?' and 'Do I attain my aim, or do I miss the mark?' In this way, and in this way only, he may acquit himself and free others from the work of legislation.

"Let the allottee then hold his lot upon the conditions which we have mentioned."
refashioned:
The purpose of our laws is to make citizens as happy as possible and as friendly toward one another as they can be. People who are constantly in court with each other, among whom many wrongs are committed, can never be friends -- only those among whom offenses and lawsuits are few and minor.

For this reason, gold and silver should not be permitted in the city, nor should the vulgar forms of commerce carried on through money-lending or the raising of inferior livestock. Only agricultural production should be allowed, and only so much of it as will not force us to neglect the very things that wealth is supposed to serve -- namely, the soul and body. Without physical training and education, neither soul nor body will ever be worth anything. Therefore, as we have said repeatedly, concern for wealth should occupy the lowest place in our priorities.

Every person has three fundamental interests. Properly understood, the interest in money ranks third and lowest. The interest in the body comes second. And first and highest is the interest in the soul. A state will be rightly constituted only if it distributes honors according to this scale. If any law has been enacted that places health above moral temperance, or wealth above health and temperate habits, that law is clearly wrong.

The legislator should therefore constantly ask himself: 'What is my aim?' and 'Am I achieving it, or am I missing the mark?' Only by this discipline can the legislator fulfill his duty and relieve others of the burden of further legislation.

Let each allottee hold their lot on the conditions we have described.
elegant: Our laws aim to make citizens happy and friendly. Constant litigation destroys friendship; therefore gold, silver, money-lending, and vulgar commerce must be excluded. Only agriculture is permitted, and only enough to avoid neglecting what wealth serves -- soul and body, which require education and training. Three interests govern every life: soul (highest), body (middle), wealth (lowest). Any law preferring health to temperance or wealth to health is wrong. The legislator must constantly ask: 'What is my aim? Am I hitting the mark?' Let each allottee hold their lot on these conditions.
summary: Laws should promote happiness and friendship, not litigation. Gold, silver, and vulgar commerce are excluded. The three human interests rank soul first, body second, wealth third -- and all legislation must honor this order. The legislator's constant question must be: Am I achieving my aim?
distilled: Every law must honor the hierarchy of soul over body over wealth -- any legislation that inverts this order is wrong, and the legislator must constantly check whether they are hitting their mark.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage synthesizes the entire Book V preamble: the soul-body-wealth hierarchy from 5.1-5.4, the economic restrictions from 5.26-5.27, and the pleasure-virtue argument from 5.12-5.15. The connection between litigation and enmity echoes Republic V.462a-e on unity. The restriction to agriculture reflects the physiocratic economics common in ancient thought -- Aristotle similarly privileges agriculture in Politics I.8 (1256a-b). The self-questioning legislator -- 'What do I want? Am I hitting the mark?' -- recalls the Socratic method of self-examination (Apology 38a) applied to political craft. The Greek word for 'missing the mark' (hamartanein) is also the word for 'sinning,' connecting legislative error to moral failure. The final sentence transitions from preamble to specific constitutional provisions.

narrative:
"It would be well that every man should come to the colony having all things equal; but seeing that this is not possible, and one man will have greater possessions than another, for many reasons and in particular in order to preserve equality in special crises of the state, qualifications of property must be unequal, in order that offices and contributions and distributions may be proportioned to the value of each person's wealth, and not solely to the virtue of his ancestors or himself, nor yet to the strength and beauty of his person, but also to the measure of his wealth or poverty; and so by a law of inequality, which will be in proportion to his wealth, he will receive honours and offices as equally as possible, and there will be no quarrels and disputes.

"To which end there should be four different standards appointed according to the amount of property: there should be a first and a second and a third and a fourth class, in which the citizens will be placed, and they will be called by these or similar names: they may continue in the same rank, or pass into another in any individual case, on becoming richer from being poorer, or poorer from being richer.

"The form of law which I should propose as the natural sequel would be as follows: In a state which is desirous of being saved from the greatest of all plagues -- not faction, but rather distraction -- there should exist among the citizens neither extreme poverty, nor, again, excess of wealth, for both are productive of both these evils."
refashioned:
Ideally, every settler would arrive at the colony with equal possessions. Since this is impossible -- some will inevitably have more than others -- and particularly since preserving equality during state crises requires it, property qualifications must be unequal. Offices, tax contributions, and public distributions should be apportioned not solely according to ancestral virtue, personal merit, physical strength, or beauty, but also according to the measure of each person's wealth or poverty. Through this law of proportional inequality -- calibrated to wealth -- each citizen will receive honors and offices as equitably as possible, minimizing quarrels and disputes.

To implement this, four property classes should be established -- first, second, third, and fourth -- into which citizens are sorted. Citizens may remain in their class or move to another as their fortunes rise or fall.

The natural law to follow from this arrangement is: any state that wishes to be saved from the greatest plague -- not mere faction, but complete social disintegration -- must ensure that neither extreme poverty nor excessive wealth exists among its citizens, since both conditions produce these catastrophic evils.
elegant: Perfect equality among settlers is impossible, so proportional inequality is necessary. Offices, taxes, and distributions should be apportioned by wealth as well as virtue and merit, creating four property classes through which citizens may rise or fall. This proportional system minimizes disputes. The essential law: a state seeking to avoid the greatest plague -- not mere faction but total social disintegration -- must prevent both extreme poverty and excessive wealth, since both produce catastrophe.
summary: Since perfect equality is impossible, four property classes should proportion offices and contributions to wealth. Citizens may move between classes as fortunes change. The essential law: neither extreme poverty nor extreme wealth should exist, as both produce social disintegration.
distilled: Four property classes distribute offices and obligations proportionally, and the state must prevent both extreme poverty and excessive wealth to avoid social disintegration.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The four property classes recall Solon's Athenian census classes (pentakosiomedimnoi, hippeis, zeugitai, thetes) -- a real historical precedent for the Laws' proposal. The principle of proportional equality (geometric rather than arithmetic) echoes Gorgias 508a and connects to Aristotle's later distinction in Politics III.9 (1280a) and NE V.3 (1131a-1132b). The greatest plague being 'distraction' (diaspasis) rather than mere faction (stasis) is a striking distinction -- civil war is not the worst outcome; complete social fragmentation is. The prohibition of extremes of wealth and poverty echoes Republic IV.421d-422a (neither wealth nor poverty for guardians) and anticipates the specific wealth limits in 5.32. The mobility between classes represents a more flexible system than the Republic's fixed class structure.

narrative:
"Now the legislator should determine what is to be the limit of poverty or wealth. Let the limit of poverty be the value of the lot; this ought to be preserved, and no ruler, nor any one else who aspires after a reputation for virtue, will allow the lot to be impaired in any case. This the legislator gives as a measure, and he will permit a man to acquire double or triple, or as much as four times the amount of this.

"But if a person have yet greater riches, whether he has found them, or they have been given to him, or he has made them in business, or has acquired by any stroke of fortune that which is in excess of the measure, if he give back the surplus to the state, and to the Gods who are the patrons of the state, he shall suffer no penalty or loss of reputation; but if he disobeys this our law any one who likes may inform against him and receive half the value of the excess, and the delinquent shall pay a sum equal to the excess out of his own property, and the other half of the excess shall belong to the Gods.

"And let every possession of every man, with the exception of the lot, be publicly registered before the magistrates whom the law appoints, so that all suits about money may be easy and quite simple."
refashioned:
The legislator must establish the boundaries of poverty and wealth. The minimum -- the floor below which no one should fall -- is the value of the original land allotment. This must be preserved in every case; no ruler or person aspiring to virtue should ever allow an allotment to be diminished. This serves as the baseline measure. A citizen may acquire up to double, triple, or as much as four times this amount.

If someone comes to possess wealth exceeding four times the lot's value -- whether through discovery, gifts, business success, or any stroke of fortune -- they must return the surplus to the state and to the gods who are the state's patrons. If they do so willingly, they suffer no penalty or loss of reputation. But if they violate this law, any citizen may bring a charge against them and receive half the value of the excess. The offender must pay from their own property a sum equal to the excess, and the other half goes to the gods.

Furthermore, every citizen's possessions, except the lot itself, must be publicly registered with the legally appointed magistrates, so that all financial disputes may be resolved easily and straightforwardly.
elegant: The minimum wealth is the value of the lot -- this must never be diminished. The maximum is four times the lot's value. Any surplus must be returned to the state and the gods without penalty. Violators face prosecution by any citizen, who receives half the excess as reward; the offender pays the full excess from personal funds, with the other half going to the gods. All possessions except the lot must be publicly registered with magistrates, ensuring simple resolution of financial disputes.
summary: The lot's value sets the poverty floor; four times the lot sets the wealth ceiling. Surplus must be returned to the state and gods. Violators face citizen prosecution, with the informer receiving half the excess. All property except the lot must be publicly registered.
distilled: Wealth is capped at four times the value of the original lot -- surplus must be returned to the state, and public property registration ensures transparency and simple dispute resolution.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 4:1 wealth ratio is remarkably specific and relatively modest by ancient standards -- Aristotle in Politics II.7 (1266b) discusses and critiques Plato's property limits. The whistleblower incentive (informer receives half the excess) adapts the Athenian legal institution of phasis, where private prosecutors could receive a portion of fines. The public property registry anticipates modern wealth transparency requirements. The preservation of the lot as an inviolable minimum creates a de facto social safety net. Cf. Republic III.416d-417b (absolute prohibition on private wealth for guardians) -- the Laws' four-tier system is a pragmatic compromise that allows inequality within defined bounds. The dedication of surplus to the gods sacralizes the wealth limit, making violation both a legal and religious offense.

narrative:
"The next thing to be noted is, that the city should be placed as nearly as possible in the centre of the country; we should choose a place which possesses what is suitable for a city, and this may easily be imagined and described. Then we will divide the city into twelve portions, first founding temples to Hestia, to Zeus and to Athene, in a spot which we will call the Acropolis, and surround with a circular wall, making the division of the entire city and country radiate from this point.

"The twelve portions shall be equalized by the provision that those which are of good land shall be smaller, while those of inferior quality shall be larger. The number of the lots shall be 5040, and each of them shall be divided into two, and every allotment shall be composed of two such sections; one of land near the city, the other of land which is at a distance. This arrangement shall be carried out in the following manner: The section which is near the city shall be added to that which is on the borders, and form one lot, and the portion which is next nearest shall be added to the portion which is next farthest; and so of the rest.

"Moreover, in the two sections of the lots the same principle of equalization of the soil ought to be maintained; the badness and goodness shall be compensated by more and less. And the legislator shall divide the citizens into twelve parts, and arrange the rest of their property, as far as possible, so as to form twelve equal parts; and there shall be a registration of all.

"After this they shall assign twelve lots to twelve Gods, and call them by their names, and dedicate to each God their several portions, and call the tribes after them. And they shall distribute the twelve divisions of the city in the same way in which they divided the country; and every man shall have two habitations, one in the centre of the country, and the other at the extremity. Enough of the manner of settlement."
refashioned:
The city should be situated as close to the center of the territory as possible, in a location possessing the qualities suitable for an urban center. The city is then divided into twelve portions. First, temples to Hestia, Zeus, and Athena are founded on a site we shall call the Acropolis, enclosed within a circular wall. The entire division of city and countryside radiates from this central point.

The twelve portions are equalized by making those with superior soil smaller and those with inferior soil larger. The 5,040 lots are each split into two halves: one section near the city and one at the territory's edge. The nearest urban section is paired with the farthest border section to form one lot; the next-nearest pairs with the next-farthest, and so on throughout.

The same equalizing principle applies within each lot's two sections -- inferior soil is compensated by greater area. The legislator shall also divide the citizens into twelve groups and distribute the rest of their property as equally as possible, registering everything.

Twelve lots are then assigned to twelve patron gods, named after them, with each portion dedicated to its respective deity. The tribes take their names from these gods. The twelve urban divisions mirror the twelve country divisions, and every citizen receives two residences -- one in the center and one at the borders. That concludes the plan of settlement.
elegant: The city sits at the territory's center, radiating outward from an Acropolis enclosed by a circular wall, with temples to Hestia, Zeus, and Athena at its heart. The entire city and countryside are divided into twelve portions, equalized so that better land receives smaller allotments and worse land larger ones. Each of the 5,040 lots is split into two halves: one section near the city paired with one at the territorial border, with soil quality compensated by adjusting area. The nearest section pairs with the farthest, the next-nearest with the next-farthest, and so on throughout. Citizens are divided into twelve tribes, each named after one of twelve patron gods to whom their respective territorial portions are dedicated. The twelve urban divisions mirror the twelve country divisions. All property is registered. Each citizen maintains two residences -- one in the center of the country, the other at its extremity.
summary: The city is centered in the territory with an Acropolis dedicated to Hestia, Zeus, and Athena. Twelve portions are equalized by compensating soil quality with size. Each of 5,040 lots has two sections paired by proximity and distance. Twelve tribes are named after patron gods, and each citizen has two residences.
distilled: The city radiates from a central Acropolis, divided into twelve god-named tribal portions with equalized lots that each span both the city center and the territory's borders.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The centrally planned city radiating from an Acropolis reflects idealized Greek city planning -- cf. Hippodamus of Miletus, whom Aristotle discusses in Politics II.8 (1267b) as the first to theorize city planning. The three Acropolis deities -- Hestia (hearth/home), Zeus (sovereign authority), Athena (wisdom/craft) -- represent the foundations of civic life. The twelve-tribe system echoes the Cleisthenic reforms in Athens (508 BC), which created ten tribes, and the twelve tribes of Israel. The pairing of near and far sections to equalize lots is an ingenious solution to the problem of geographic inequality. The dual residence requirement -- center and periphery -- ensures that citizens have stakes in both urban and border areas, distributing defensive responsibility. The number twelve connects to the twelve Olympian gods and the twelve divisions established in 5.20-5.21.

narrative:
"Now we ought by all means to consider that there can never be such a happy concurrence of circumstances as we have described; neither can all things coincide as they are wanted. Men who will not take offence at such a mode of living together, and will endure all their life long to have their property fixed at a moderate limit, and to beget children in accordance with our ordinances, and will allow themselves to be deprived of gold and other things which the legislator, as is evident from these enactments, will certainly forbid them; and will endure, further, the situation of the land with the city in the middle and dwellings round about -- all this is as if the legislator were telling his dreams, or making a city and citizens of wax.

"There is truth in these objections, and therefore every one should take to heart what I am going to say. Once more, then, the legislator shall appear and address us: 'O my friends,' he will say to us, 'do not suppose me ignorant that there is a certain degree of truth in your words; but I am of opinion that, in matters which are not present but future, he who exhibits a pattern of that at which he aims, should in nothing fall short of the fairest and truest; and that if he finds any part of this work impossible of execution he should avoid and not execute it, but he should contrive to carry out that which is nearest and most akin to it; you must allow the legislator to perfect his design, and when it is perfected, you should join with him in considering what part of his legislation is expedient and what will arouse opposition; for surely the artist who is to be deemed worthy of any regard at all, ought always to make his work self-consistent.'"
refashioned:
We must frankly acknowledge that such a perfect alignment of circumstances as we have described can never actually occur. Not everything will fall into place as desired. Finding people who will accept without complaint this mode of communal life -- who will endure fixed property limits throughout their lives, bear children according to regulation, surrender gold and other things the legislator will obviously prohibit, and tolerate the prescribed arrangement of land with the city at center and dwellings radiating outward -- all of this sounds as though the legislator were recounting a dream, or modeling a city and its citizens out of wax.

These objections contain real truth, and everyone should take seriously what follows. The legislator speaks again: 'My friends, do not imagine I am unaware of the truth in your words. But I hold that when designing something for the future rather than the present, the person presenting a pattern of what they aim at should make that pattern as fair and true as possible, without shortfall. Where any part of the design proves impossible to execute, it should be set aside. But the nearest practicable approximation should be pursued instead. Allow the legislator to complete the full design first. Once it is finished, join in evaluating which parts are expedient and which will provoke resistance. An artist who deserves any regard at all must always keep their work internally consistent.'
elegant: We must honestly admit that such perfect circumstances can never be realized -- the scheme sounds like a legislator's dream or a city modeled in wax. These objections are fair. But the legislator responds: in designing the future, present the fullest and truest pattern possible. Where execution proves impossible, set that part aside and pursue the nearest approximation. Complete the design first, then evaluate what is practical and what will face resistance. Any artist worthy of respect must keep their work self-consistent.
summary: The Athenian acknowledges that the proposed arrangements are idealistic -- like a legislator's dream. But the legislator defends the method: design the fullest possible pattern first, then identify what is achievable and approximate the rest. The design must be internally consistent.
distilled: Present the ideal design in full first -- then set aside what proves impossible and pursue the nearest achievable approximation, always maintaining internal consistency.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, voicing a self-aware objection and the legislator's response. The 'city of wax' objection echoes the challenge in Republic V.472c-473b (is the ideal state possible?) and Republic IX.592a-b (the ideal state as a 'pattern laid up in heaven'). The method described -- design the ideal, then approximate -- is Plato's mature political methodology, representing a significant evolution from the Republic's more uncompromising idealism. The artist/craftsman analogy for the legislator connects to the Demiurge in the Timaeus (28a-29b), who looks to an eternal pattern and copies it as closely as the material allows. The principle of self-consistency (to homologoumenon) is central to Platonic dialectic -- cf. Gorgias 482b-c on the importance of not contradicting oneself.

narrative:
"Having determined that there is to be a distribution into twelve parts, let us now see in what way this may be accomplished. There is no difficulty in perceiving that the twelve parts admit of the greatest number of divisions of that which they include, or in seeing the other numbers which are consequent upon them, and are produced out of them up to 5040; wherefore the law ought to order phratries and demes and villages, and also military ranks and movements, as well as coins and measures, dry and liquid, and weights, so as to be commensurable and agreeable to one another.

"Nor should we fear the appearance of minuteness, if the law commands that all the vessels which a man possesses should have a common measure, when we consider generally that the divisions and variations of numbers have a use in respect of all the variations of which they are susceptible, both in themselves and as measures of height and depth, and in all sounds, and in motions, as well those which proceed in a straight direction, upwards or downwards, as in those which go round and round.

"The legislator is to consider all these things and to bid the citizens, as far as possible, not to lose sight of numerical order; for no single instrument of youthful education has such mighty power, both as regards domestic economy and politics, and in the arts, as the study of arithmetic. Above all, arithmetic stirs up him who is by nature sleepy and dull, and makes him quick to learn, retentive, shrewd, and aided by art divine he makes progress quite beyond his natural powers.

"All such things, if only the legislator, by other laws and institutions, can banish meanness and covetousness from the souls of men, so that they can use them properly and to their own good, will be excellent and suitable instruments of education."
refashioned:
Having established a twelvefold division, let us consider how to implement it. It is easy to see that twelve admits the greatest number of subdivisions, and that the numbers generated from it up to 5,040 offer extraordinary flexibility. Therefore the law should organize clans, districts, and villages, as well as military formations and maneuvers, together with coinage, measures for dry and liquid goods, and weights, so that all these systems are mutually commensurable and harmonious.

We should not worry about seeming overly detailed if the law requires all household vessels to conform to a common standard of measurement. The divisions and variations of numbers have applications across every domain -- dimensions of height and depth, musical intervals, every kind of motion whether linear or rotational.

The legislator must attend to all of this and instruct citizens never to lose sight of numerical order. No single instrument of education holds as much power as the study of arithmetic -- in household management, in politics, and in the arts alike.
